proposals.title: Distributed Bottleneck Fairness: A Formal and Experimental Challenge to the Single-Server Fair Share Model
proposals.motivation: The canonical Fair Share (e.g., Generalized Processor Sharing) model and its practical approximations (e.g., DRR, SFQ) assume a single, centralized bottleneck resource. Modern distributed systems, from disaggregated data centers to federated learning clusters, inherently feature multiple, dynamic, and distributed congestion points. It is unclear whether the fairness guarantees of these classic algorithms hold, or how they compose, in such multi-bottleneck environments, posing a fundamental challenge to performance isolation and service-level agreements.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that in networks with multiple, distributed bottlenecks, the end-to-end fairness properties of classic Fair Share schedulers degrade or become unpredictable due to complex queueing interactions, and that a new, data-centric definition of distributed fairness is required.
proposals.method: We will first construct a formal model of distributed bottlenecks, analyzing the composition of Fair Share schedulers (e.g., SFQ) using network calculus. We will then design and implement a testbed using programmable switches (P4) and software endpoints to create controlled, multi-bottleneck topologies. The core experiment will measure flow completion time fairness and throughput allocation for competing flows under varying bottleneck placements and loads, contrasting results with single-server model predictions.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first rigorous analysis and empirical validation of Fair Share scheduling in distributed bottleneck scenarios. It will yield a concrete framework for evaluating distributed fairness and could inform the design of next-generation, coordination-aware scheduling protocols for disaggregated infrastructure.
proposals.novelty: While prior work analyzes fairness at a single node or in specific topologies, this proposal directly challenges the foundational single-server assumption itself, systematically investigating the emergent behavior of these well-understood algorithms in a generalized, distributed setting—a critical gap as systems architecture moves away from centralized models.

proposals.title: Fair Share in the Multi-Server Jungle: A Topology-Aware Analysis for Data-Centric Networks
proposals.motivation: The Fair Share (FS) discipline, foundational to congestion control in TCP and core to the design of fair queuing in single-server settings, lacks a rigorous analysis for modern data-centric networks. These networks feature complex topologies with multiple, heterogeneous servers (e.g., in data centers, edge computing, and distributed trust systems), where naive FS application can lead to severe performance degradation and unfairness. Understanding FS's behavior in these topologies is critical for building predictable, high-performance distributed services.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a topology-aware formulation of Fair Share, which accounts for server capacities, flow paths, and network contention points, can be derived and will provably outperform both classical FS and existing heuristic multi-server schedulers in terms of fairness, throughput, and flow completion time.
proposals.method: We will first develop a formal model of a multi-server network as a directed graph with servers as nodes and links as edges, each with a capacity. We will define a global fairness objective, extending the max-min fairness principle to this topology. Our technical approach will involve designing a distributed algorithm, inspired by recent work on distributed trust and consensus, where servers exchange flow state information to iteratively compute a topology-aware fair rate. We will analyze its convergence properties and implement a simulation framework to evaluate it against baselines (e.g., per-server FS, DRF) on canonical data center topologies (e.g., fat-tree, leaf-spine).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first principled, generalized definition of Fair Share for arbitrary network topologies. It will yield a practical, distributed scheduling algorithm for data-centric networks, directly contributing to the design of next-generation congestion control and resource allocation protocols. The analysis will also offer new insights into the inherent trade-offs between local and global fairness in distributed systems.
proposals.novelty: While Fair Share is well-understood for a single bottleneck, its systematic extension to multi-server graphs with interdependent capacities is an open and non-trivial problem. Our work moves beyond treating servers in isolation, explicitly modeling the network topology as a first-class constraint in the fairness optimization, a perspective largely absent in pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: Internet-Scale Evaluation of Fair Share: A Simulation Framework for Global Congestion Control
proposals.motivation: The Fair Share congestion control discipline, which allocates bottleneck bandwidth equally among flows, is a theoretically elegant solution for network fairness and stability. However, its performance and deployment implications have not been rigorously evaluated at the scale and heterogeneity of the global internet. Pre-2026, the networking community lacks the tools and analysis to understand how such a fundamental shift in congestion management would behave across realistic topologies with diverse traffic patterns and competing protocols.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a global deployment of the Fair Share discipline would significantly reduce flow completion times for the majority of small flows and improve overall network utilization, but may introduce new stability challenges at peering points and in deeply nested congestion scenarios.
proposals.method: We will develop a large-scale simulation framework by extending the ns-3 simulator with a precise implementation of the Fair Share algorithm. We will construct internet-scale topologies using data from projects like CAIDA's AS relationships and integrate realistic traffic matrices from prior measurement studies. The core of our method involves comparative simulations against standard TCP variants (CUBIC, BBR) and existing AQM schemes (CoDel, PIE) under varying load conditions, measuring key metrics like flow completion time distribution, link utilization, and fairness indices.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first comprehensive, data-driven assessment of Fair Share's viability as an internet-wide congestion control principle. It will yield a publicly available, high-fidelity simulation framework for evaluating future congestion control schemes at scale and identify critical deployment challenges and necessary protocol modifications.
proposals.novelty: While Fair Share is well-understood in abstract or small-scale settings, the novelty lies in scaling the evaluation to internet-level complexity, explicitly modeling the interaction with legacy traffic, and quantifying the systemic effects—both benefits and instability risks—that cannot be extrapolated from simpler models.

proposals.title: A Testbed Evaluation of Accountable Internet Protocols: Performance and Security Under Realistic Workloads
proposals.motivation: The Internet's original design lacks intrinsic accountability, making it vulnerable to misbehavior like prefix hijacking and DDoS attacks. Accountable Internet Protocols (AIP), proposed as a clean-slate architecture, promise to embed verifiable accountability into the network layer. However, its performance overhead and security guarantees have only been analyzed theoretically or in simulation, lacking validation in a realistic, distributed environment.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a carefully engineered AIP prototype can be deployed on a physical testbed with acceptable performance overhead for realistic traffic patterns while successfully detecting and mitigating specified in-network attacks.
proposals.method: We will build a prototype AIP network on the CloudLab testbed, implementing its core data-centric forwarding and auditing mechanisms. The system will use a distributed ledger (e.g., a permissioned blockchain) for maintaining the binding between self-certifying data identifiers and locators, as outlined in prior AIP literature. We will develop traffic generators to emulate web and data-center workloads, and instrument the network to measure latency, throughput, and auditing overhead. Security claims will be tested by launching controlled prefix hijacking and route manipulation attacks within the testbed.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first comprehensive, empirical evaluation of AIP's core trade-offs between accountability, performance, and deployability. It will yield a publicly available, reproducible testbed blueprint and dataset, serving as a crucial benchmark for future research on accountable networking architectures.
proposals.novelty: While AIP's design is known, its practical evaluation has been limited. This proposal moves beyond simulation to a controlled but realistic distributed testbed, assessing the protocol's behavior under genuine concurrency, partial failures, and adversarial conditions—a critical step prior to any real-world deployment.

proposals.title: Fast and Consistent Key Revocation for Hierarchical Attestation Infrastructures
proposals.motivation: The proliferation of hardware-based attestation, such as Intel SGX and AMD SEV, creates a critical dependency on a hierarchical chain of trust (AIP). Compromised keys in this hierarchy can undermine the security of millions of enclaves. Pre-2026, revocation mechanisms in these systems are often slow, relying on periodic Certificate Revocation List (CRL) updates, creating a dangerous window of vulnerability for distributed services.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a data-centric networking approach, treating revocation state as a high-priority, immutable data object, can enable near-instantaneous, consistent revocation across a global distributed system, bounded primarily by propagation delay rather than periodic update cycles.
proposals.method: We will design a protocol inspired by distributed consensus and fast data dissemination techniques. The core is a logically centralized but physically distributed revocation service that publishes signed, versioned revocation statements. These statements are disseminated via an overlay network optimized for low-latency, reliable delivery, using techniques akin to reliable multicast and epidemic propagation. Clients and intermediate attestation services subscribe to a continuous stream of these statements, enabling immediate local policy enforcement upon receipt, bypassing traditional CRL pull mechanisms.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a concrete, implementable protocol for high-performance distributed trust maintenance, significantly reducing the mean time to revocation. It will contribute a formal model for analyzing revocation consistency and latency trade-offs in hierarchical trust systems, advancing the field of data-centric security infrastructure.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in applying data-centric networking principles—treating revocation not as a configuration update but as a critical, real-time data stream—to the specific problem of attestation infrastructure, moving beyond the pull-based models of PKI and traditional certificate transparency logs.

proposals.title: On the Scalability of Self-Certifying Address Hierarchies: A Formal Model for Internet-Scale Growth
proposals.motivation: The Internet's continued growth, driven by IoT and edge computing, places immense pressure on its core naming and addressing infrastructure. Self-certifying address schemes, like those in AIP (Accountable Internet Protocol) and other data-centric architectures, promise enhanced security and trust but rely on hierarchical structures whose scaling properties under exponential growth are not formally understood. This creates a critical gap in evaluating the long-term viability of next-generation network architectures.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the hierarchical self-certifying address space, as proposed in data-centric networking paradigms, exhibits fundamental scaling limits under realistic Internet growth models, leading to either unsustainable routing table growth or unacceptable delegation path lengths.
proposals.method: We will construct a formal graph-theoretic model of the hierarchical address delegation tree, parameterized by delegation policy and growth rate. Using analytical techniques from distributed systems and stochastic processes, we will derive bounds on state (e.g., routing table size) and path length as functions of the number of endpoints. We will validate the model via discrete-event simulation, using projected growth trajectories from pre-2026 IoT forecasts, and compare against the scaling of traditional IP and other overlay-based trust systems.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first rigorous scalability analysis for hierarchical self-certifying addressing, offering concrete design guidelines for future protocol architects. It will contribute a formal framework for evaluating trade-offs between trust, decentralization, and scalability in data-centric network designs.
proposals.novelty: While prior work on AIP and data-centric networking focuses on functionality and security, a formal, predictive model of its hierarchical addressing scalability under Internet-scale growth is novel. The analysis bridges the gap between abstract trust architectures and the concrete systems constraints of a planetary-scale network.

proposals.title: Accountability in Private Networks: A Practical Implementation of AIP Principles for Internal Service Auditing
proposals.motivation: As corporate and data center networks grow in complexity, internal service failures and policy violations are difficult to trace and attribute, hindering debugging and security. Pre-2026 work on Accountability in Internet Protocols (AIP) offers a powerful conceptual framework for network-wide accountability but remains largely theoretical and focused on wide-area Internet scenarios. A practical implementation for controlled, high-performance private networks is a critical missing piece.
proposals.hypothesis: The core AIP principles of maintaining a tamper-evident, network-wide diagnostic log can be implemented efficiently within a private network's existing control plane, providing precise, actionable accountability for internal service interactions without significant performance overhead.
proposals.method: We will design a lightweight AIP agent integrated with the network's distributed control plane (e.g., based on a logically centralized controller like in SDN). This agent will use a deterministic, collision-resistant hash chain to link network events (e.g., RPCs, state changes) reported by instrumented services and switches. We will develop a 'spirit'-based auditing protocol, inspired by Shenker's recent work on trust, where a minimal set of cryptographic attestations ('statements') are propagated and verified in-band with data packets, creating an immutable audit trail. The system will be evaluated on a testbed using microservices and distributed storage workloads.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first concrete architecture and implementation of AIP for a private network context, demonstrating its feasibility and value for internal accountability. It will yield a set of design principles and a reference implementation for adding forensic capability to data center and corporate networks, advancing the field from theory to practice.
proposals.novelty: While AIP is known, its adaptation to the unique constraints and opportunities of private networks—where trust boundaries differ and performance is paramount—is unexplored. The novelty lies in the synthesis of AIP with modern data center control planes and the development of a 'teaching'-inspired, lightweight attestation protocol suitable for high-speed internal links.

proposals.title: Dual: A Protocol for Accountable Anonymity in Distributed Services
proposals.motivation: Modern distributed services, from financial ledgers to federated learning, require a balance between user privacy and system accountability. Existing systems often force a trade-off: strong anonymity (e.g., mixnets) sacrifices the ability to hold users accountable for malicious actions, while accountable systems (e.g., signed logs) reveal identities. This gap hinders the deployment of privacy-preserving yet trustworthy services. The problem is acute in high-stakes distributed trust environments where participation must be provably authorized but actions should not be linkable to real-world identities.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by combining group signatures with a novel, decentralized credential issuance and revocation mechanism, we can construct a practical network protocol that provides strong anonymity for authorized actions while enabling efficient, cryptographic accountability for policy violations.
proposals.method: We will design the Dual protocol around a dynamic, decentralized group manager implemented as a Byzantine Fault Tolerant (BFT) service, inspired by recent high-performance distributed trust architectures. This manager will issue and revoke anonymous credentials using a variant of the Boneh-Shacham group signature scheme. The core technical challenge is designing a lightweight, data-centric networking layer that propagates and verifies authorization proofs and revocation lists without creating metadata side-channels, extending principles from data-centric networking research. We will formally model the system's security properties using the Universal Composability framework.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a formally specified and verifiable protocol for accountable anonymity, 2) an open-source implementation and performance evaluation demonstrating feasibility for high-throughput services, and 3) a concrete framework for teaching the principles of privacy-accountability trade-offs in distributed systems, aligning with the pedagogical spirit of recent work.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the integration of a BFT-managed, dynamic group signature scheme into a data-centric network protocol stack, explicitly designed to minimize metadata leakage while providing real-time accountability—a synthesis not addressed by prior anonymity or accountability systems in isolation.

proposals.title: Sharding the Global AIP Namespace: A Scalable Architecture for Inter-Domain Information-Centric Networking
proposals.motivation: The vision of a global-scale, inter-domain Architecture for an Information Plane (AIP) faces a fundamental scalability challenge: a flat namespace of information objects would require routing tables that grow with the number of objects, which is infeasible. This problem mirrors the scaling issues of early inter-domain routing (e.g., BGP table growth) but is exacerbated by the finer granularity of information-centric identifiers. Without a scalable solution, the practical deployment of a global AIP remains out of reach.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that partitioning the global AIP namespace into administratively scoped shards, and designing a corresponding shard-level routing and resolution system, can manage routing table growth while preserving the data-centric service model and enabling efficient, scalable inter-domain information retrieval.
proposals.method: We will design a sharding scheme based on a hierarchical, self-certifying naming structure (e.g., derived from public keys or provider prefixes) to enable deterministic shard assignment. We will develop a two-tier resolution system: 1) a scalable inter-domain shard routing protocol, inspired by ideas from distributed hash tables and locator/identifier separation, to advertise shard reachability, and 2) intra-shard resolution mechanisms for object lookup within a shard's domain of authority. The architecture will be evaluated via simulation and emulation on testbeds like CloudLab, measuring routing table size, lookup latency, and resilience under churn.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first concrete architectural blueprint for a scalable, inter-domain AIP, directly addressing the critical routing scalability barrier. It will contribute a novel sharding design and associated protocols that balance scalability, autonomy, and efficient data retrieval, advancing the field of information-centric and data-centric networking from intra-domain prototypes towards a viable global system.
proposals.novelty: While sharding is known in distributed systems, its application to the inter-domain AIP problem is novel, requiring a synthesis of networking principles (like scalable routing) with distributed systems techniques (like consistent hashing and resolution), all while adhering to the data-centric service model's core tenets of security and decentralization.

proposals.title: P4C: A Compiler-Optimized Policy-to-Dataplane Pipeline for Distributed Trust
proposals.motivation: Modern distributed systems and data-centric networks rely on complex, high-level policy rules for security, routing, and resource management. However, current methods for deploying these policies, such as in distributed trust frameworks or SDN controllers, often generate inefficient, bloated forwarding tables, leading to poor performance and scalability. This inefficiency contradicts the spirit of high-performance distributed systems design.
proposals.hypothesis: By treating high-level policy rules as a domain-specific language (DSL) and applying compiler optimization techniques—such as constant propagation, dead code elimination, and common subexpression elimination—we can automatically generate minimal, optimal forwarding table entries for distributed network elements.
proposals.method: We will design a new compiler, P4C (Policy-to-Plan Compiler), that ingests a high-level policy DSL. The compiler front-end will parse and construct an intermediate representation (IR) graph of policy rules. The optimizer will apply a series of passes, inspired by LLVM, to simplify and merge rules across a distributed topology. The back-end will target efficient P4 code and compressed forwarding table entries, leveraging Shenker's recent work on data-centric abstractions and distributed trust to define the compilation context and target architecture.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a principled, automated toolchain for translating intent into efficient network state, directly improving the performance of systems built for distributed trust. It will provide a formal bridge between the 'what' of policy and the 'place' of its distributed execution, advancing the field of data-centric networking.
proposals.novelty: While compilers for network configurations exist, applying deep, multi-pass compiler optimizations specifically to the problem of policy minimization for distributed trust and forwarding is a novel synthesis. It moves beyond simple rule translation to a holistic optimization of the entire policy-to-dataplane pipeline.

proposals.title: Dual: A Query-Centric Architecture for Distributed Network Policy Enforcement
proposals.motivation: Modern networks enforce complex, dynamic policies (e.g., access control, QoS, routing) across distributed components. Current approaches, like SDN, centralize policy decisions or distribute them via ad-hoc protocols, leading to latency, consistency issues, and complexity in verifying end-to-end behavior. A fundamental re-conceptualization is needed to make policy enforcement as reliable and analyzable as querying a database.
proposals.hypothesis: By modeling a data packet's traversal as a series of queries to a logically centralized, distributed policy state, we can achieve verifiable, high-performance enforcement with strong consistency guarantees, simplifying network reasoning and management.
proposals.method: We will design the Dual architecture, where each packet header is a query key. Network elements (switches, middleboxes) act as query processors over a distributed, replicated policy store built using a high-performance consensus protocol (e.g., a variant of Raft or EPaxos). We will develop a query language for policies and a compiler that translates high-level intents into distributed query plans. The core challenge is optimizing the query-path to meet line-rate forwarding, leveraging techniques from in-network computing and hardware-accelerated key-value stores.
proposals.contribution: This work will yield a new architectural blueprint for policy-centric networking that decouples policy definition from enforcement mechanics. It will provide a formal framework for verifying network-wide policy compliance and a practical system demonstrating that strong consistency does not preclude high performance in distributed trust environments.
proposals.novelty: While prior work separates control and data planes, Dual uniquely treats the data plane itself as a distributed query evaluation engine over explicit policy state, merging concepts from distributed databases and networking in a novel way to guarantee correct, analyzable packet treatment.

proposals.title: A Formal Semantics for Flat Service and Endpoint Identifiers in Data-Centric Architectures
proposals.motivation: The shift towards data-centric networking and distributed trust models, as seen in prior work on Fast Networks and Data Centric Networking, requires a fundamental rethinking of naming. Current hierarchical, location-bound identifiers (like IP addresses) conflate identity, location, and access semantics, hindering service mobility, security, and efficient distributed computation. A clean-slate, formally specified identifier layer is a prerequisite for the next generation of high-performance, trustworthy distributed systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a formally defined, flat identifier space for services and endpoints, with explicit semantics for structure, attributes, and binding, can provide a more robust, secure, and flexible foundation for distributed system communication than current addressing schemes.
proposals.method: We will develop a formal model using process calculus (e.g., a π-calculus variant) to define the core semantics of flat, self-certifying identifiers. This model will specify the structure of identifiers as cryptographic hashes of public keys or service descriptions, define attributes (e.g., service type, required capabilities), and formally encode binding rules for discovery, resolution, and secure communication. The semantics will be implemented and evaluated within a simulated data-centric network stack to verify properties like consistency under churn and resilience to impersonation attacks.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first rigorous formal foundation for flat identifiers in data-centric networks, enabling precise reasoning about system properties. It will yield a concrete specification that can guide the implementation of future network architectures, directly advancing the goals of distributed trust and service-oriented communication.
proposals.novelty: While flat identifiers exist (e.g., in distributed hash tables), a comprehensive formal semantics defining their structure, attributes, and binding rules in the context of service-oriented, data-centric architectures is novel. This moves beyond ad-hoc designs to a principled framework grounded in formal methods.

proposals.title: A Practical Migration Path from DNS to a Layered Naming Architecture
proposals.motivation: The Domain Name System (DNS) is a foundational but ossified global service, conflating naming, addressing, and service discovery. Its monolithic design impedes innovation in security, privacy, and functionality, as seen in ongoing debates about DNSSEC and encrypted DNS. A layered architecture, separating identifiers from locators and attributes, offers a cleaner design but presents a massive deployment challenge.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a carefully designed migration path, leveraging incremental deployment and backward compatibility, can transition the Internet from DNS to a layered naming architecture without requiring a 'flag day' or breaking existing services.
proposals.method: We will design a 'shim' compatibility layer that translates between legacy DNS queries/responses and a new layered protocol (e.g., inspired by Data-Oriented Networking or flat-label proposals). This involves: 1) developing a new resolver library that first attempts a lookup in the layered system, falling back to DNS, 2) creating a mechanism for dual-stack authoritative servers that can serve both protocols, and 3) designing economic and trust incentives (inspired by distributed trust research) for early adopters to deploy the new infrastructure. The transition plan will be validated via simulation and a testbed deployment.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first concrete, operationally viable blueprint for evolving the Internet's naming infrastructure. It will contribute a set of compatibility mechanisms and a staged deployment strategy, moving the discussion of future naming from pure architecture to practical systems engineering.
proposals.novelty: While layered architectures have been proposed, the novelty lies in the systematic focus on the *transition* problem itself, applying a 'data-centric' and 'distributed trust' mindset to create a pragmatic, incentive-compatible migration path rather than just a clean-slate design.

proposals.title: Epidemic Caching: Gossip Protocols for Consistent, Scalable Resolution of Flat Identifiers
proposals.motivation: Future internet architectures, such as those based on data-centric networking (DCN) or flat identifier schemes, require scalable and robust resolution services. Traditional hierarchical or quorum-based consistency mechanisms for resolution caches create performance bottlenecks and single points of failure, undermining the distributed spirit of these new architectures. A lightweight, decentralized mechanism is needed to maintain eventual consistency across large, dynamic sets of resolution caches.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that epidemic (gossip) protocols, adapted for the specific consistency semantics of identifier-to-locator bindings, can provide a scalable and fault-tolerant foundation for resolution cache consistency, outperforming traditional coordination-based methods in large-scale, unreliable environments.
proposals.method: We will design a novel gossip-based cache consistency protocol tailored for flat identifier namespaces. The method involves: 1) Defining a versioned data structure for bindings and a merge function to resolve concurrent updates, inspired by conflict-free replicated data types (CRDTs). 2) Implementing an adaptive gossip dissemination strategy that prioritizes new or updated bindings, leveraging lessons from prior work on high-performance distributed trust. 3) Evaluating the protocol via simulation and a testbed deployment, measuring consistency convergence time, message overhead, and robustness to node churn against a baseline using a centralized or Paxos-based coordination service.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a practical, analytically-grounded design for maintaining eventually consistent resolution caches at internet scale. It will advance the field of data-centric and service-centric networking by providing a critical missing building block. The evaluation will yield concrete guidance on parameter tuning and deployment trade-offs for system builders.
proposals.novelty: While gossip is well-studied for membership and aggregate computation, its application to the specific consistency and performance requirements of a global-scale resolution cache for flat identifiers is novel. The key innovation lies in adapting epidemic semantics to the unique update patterns and low-latency lookup needs of a foundational naming service.

proposals.title: Enterprise-Scale Service Fabrics: A Context-Limited Architecture for High-Performance, Trustworthy Networking
proposals.motivation: The vision of a global, flat namespace for services and endpoints, as in architectures like the Service-Oriented Internet, faces immense challenges in scalability, trust, and policy enforcement at planetary scale. However, within a single enterprise or cloud provider's network, these challenges are dramatically reduced due to unified administrative control and inherent trust boundaries. This creates a tractable environment to realize the performance and programmability benefits of clean-slate network abstractions.
proposals.hypothesis: By restricting the deployment of a service/endpoint identifier resolution layer to a single administrative domain, we can achieve high-performance, trustworthy distributed systems that are impractical at global scale, while simplifying network management and enabling new data-centric communication patterns.
proposals.method: We will design and implement a prototype enterprise service fabric based on pre-2026 data-centric networking principles. The architecture will feature a logically centralized but physically distributed directory service for mapping persistent service identifiers to ephemeral endpoint locators, using a consensus protocol (like Raft) for consistency within the trusted domain. Forwarding will leverage programmable switches (P4) for fast, in-network resolution and packet steering, building on lessons from prior high-performance distributed trust systems. We will evaluate the system against traditional IP-based service meshes in a testbed, measuring latency, throughput, and management complexity for microservice workloads.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a concrete, deployable blueprint for next-generation enterprise networks that decouple service logic from location. It will demonstrate how context-limited deployment of advanced abstractions can yield immediate practical benefits in performance and simplicity, offering a viable evolutionary path from current data centers. The findings will also inform the broader debate on internet architecture by clarifying which problems are truly inherent to scale versus those of administrative fragmentation.
proposals.novelty: While global clean-slate architectures are well-studied, this proposal uniquely argues for their deliberate containment within an enterprise context as a strategic design choice, not a fallback. It synthesizes Shenker's themes of service-centric networking and distributed trust with the pragmatic 'spirit' of incremental deployability, moving from theoretical 'what' to a 'teaching' example of a viable subsystem.

proposals.title: Git for Networks: A Data-Centric Framework for Verifiable Configuration Management
proposals.motivation: Modern data center and WAN networks are complex, distributed systems where configuration errors are a leading cause of outages and security breaches. Current configuration management is often ad-hoc, lacking the rigorous versioning, auditability, and collaborative workflows that software development has enjoyed for decades. This gap between network operations and software engineering practices hinders reliability and agility.
proposals.hypothesis: By modeling network-wide state (configurations, forwarding tables, ACLs) as a distributed, immutable data structure managed by a version control system (VCS) core, we can provide strong guarantees of correctness, enable safe experimentation via branching, and dramatically simplify rollback and audit.
proposals.method: We will design a new network control plane architecture where a logically centralized controller maintains a Git-like Merkle DAG of the entire network's intended state. Each commit represents a verifiable, atomic network-wide change. We will implement a prototype using the P4 language to define the data model and the ONOS controller framework, extending it with conflict detection algorithms for concurrent policy updates and efficient mechanisms for 'checking out' specific commit states to physical devices.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a formally grounded, data-centric model for network configuration that provides built-in version history, causality tracking, and non-blocking rollback. It will yield a concrete system prototype and a set of principles for 'network infrastructure as data,' advancing the field of manageable and trustworthy distributed systems.
proposals.novelty: While configuration management tools (Ansible, Puppet) and network verification tools exist, applying the full semantic model of a distributed VCS (branch/merge, immutable commits, cryptographic hashing) directly to the network control plane as a first-class primitive is a novel synthesis of distributed systems and networking principles.

proposals.title: Accelerator-Centric SubOS: Extending the SubOS Model for Heterogeneous Hardware Resource Management
proposals.motivation: Modern data centers are increasingly heterogeneous, with GPUs, FPGAs, and other accelerators becoming primary, contended resources for AI/ML and high-performance computing workloads. Existing OS abstractions and scheduling models, designed for CPU-centric sharing, fail to provide the fine-grained, performance-isolated, and secure multiplexing required for these accelerator-heavy environments, leading to underutilization and complex, ad-hoc management.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the subOS model—which delegates resource management to service-specific subsystems—can be effectively extended to manage heterogeneous accelerators as first-class, shared resources, enabling more efficient, secure, and programmable allocation than current OS or hypervisor-level approaches.
proposals.method: We will design and implement an Accelerator-Centric SubOS (AC-SubOS) architecture. The core is a minimal kernel-level 'guard' that enforces isolation and implements a low-level capability-based interface for accelerator access (e.g., GPU memory contexts, FPGA bitstreams). We will develop new 'accelerator subOS' libraries that implement high-level scheduling policies (e.g., spatial/temporal partitioning, MIG-style slicing, priority queues) for specific accelerator types, allowing services to tailor management. A key algorithm will be a distributed, two-level scheduler coordinating across CPU and accelerator subOS instances, inspired by Shenker's prior work on distributed trust and data-centric networking principles.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a new architectural framework for OS support in heterogeneous data centers, demonstrating how to treat accelerators as principal managed resources. It will provide a concrete implementation and evaluation of accelerator subOSes for at least one platform (e.g., NVIDIA GPUs), showing improved utilization, isolation, and programmability compared to baseline Linux or Kubernetes orchestration.
proposals.novelty: While prior work manages accelerators via device drivers or coarse-grained virtualization, this proposal novelly applies the subOS philosophy—decentralized, service-specific control—to the accelerator resource management problem, creating a unified but flexible model for heterogeneous hardware akin to how subOS managed network and storage resources.

proposals.title: Universal SubOS: A Data-Centric Framework for Multi-Kernel Binary Compatibility
proposals.motivation: The subOS model, as a minimal kernel abstraction for service-centric and distributed computing, has demonstrated value in Linux environments. However, modern data centers and edge networks are heterogeneous, often requiring legacy or performance-optimized Windows and BSD binaries. The inability to run these unmodified binaries within a lightweight, data-centric subOS framework limits deployment flexibility and forces reliance on heavier virtualization or dual-boot systems, contradicting the spirit of efficient, service-oriented architecture.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the subOS abstraction can be generalized into a universal, data-centric compatibility layer that can safely host unmodified binaries from disparate OS kernels (Windows NT, BSD) by systematically mediating their divergent system call semantics and kernel object models through a minimal set of principled, learning-informed policies.
proposals.method: We will first conduct a comparative analysis of system call and driver APIs across Linux, Windows NT, and FreeBSD kernels to identify a minimal, canonical set of abstractions. We will then design and implement a 'Policy Mediation Layer' that uses a combination of static binary analysis (for syscall interception points) and a lightweight, in-kernel learning model to adapt runtime resource requests (e.g., file paths, process semantics) to the host subOS environment. The core architecture will extend the 'Data Centric Networking' principle to process execution, treating binary-system interactions as structured data flows to be translated.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a practical, extensible framework for multi-OS binary compatibility within a minimal service substrate, directly enhancing distributed trust and performance by reducing environment fragmentation. It will also provide a formal methodology for analyzing and mediating cross-kernel semantic differences, advancing the field of operating system interoperability.
proposals.novelty: Unlike existing solutions (e.g., full virtualization, Wine/Cygwin), this approach uniquely seeks to embed multi-OS support within the minimal, service-oriented subOS model itself, using a data-centric and learning-informed mediation layer rather than emulation or API re-implementation.

proposals.title: VeriSup: Formally Verified Supervisor Primitives for Distributed Trust Systems
proposals.motivation: Distributed trust systems, such as those for consensus or smart contracts, rely on a critical supervisor component to manage state and enforce protocols. Bugs in these supervisors can lead to catastrophic failures, including safety violations and financial loss. Pre-2026, high-assurance systems like seL4 have demonstrated the power of formal verification for OS kernels, but these techniques have not been systematically applied to the core supervisors of modern distributed trust architectures.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the core state machine and critical operations of a distributed trust supervisor can be formally specified and verified for functional correctness and key safety properties, eliminating whole classes of runtime faults.
proposals.method: We will design a minimal supervisor for a Byzantine Fault Tolerant (BFT) consensus protocol, inspired by the architecture of systems like Hyperledger Fabric. Using the Coq proof assistant, we will formally specify its abstract state machine and key operations (e.g., commit, view change). We will then implement these operations in a verified subset (e.g., using the Verified Software Toolchain or a similar framework) and prove crucial invariants, such as agreement and state integrity, hold under the assumed fault model. The verified core will be extracted to executable code and integrated with an unverified networking layer.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first formally verified core for a distributed trust supervisor, providing a concrete artifact and proof framework for high-assurance distributed systems. It will advance the field of data-centric networking by demonstrating how to apply formal methods to the 'control plane' of trust, moving beyond performance-centric designs to provably correct ones.
proposals.novelty: While verified OS kernels exist, applying this rigorous methodology to the novel and complex state machines of distributed trust supervisors—which manage logical consensus rather than physical resources—is a significant and unexplored challenge. This bridges the 'spirit' of teaching correctness from verified systems with the practical needs of high-performance distributed services.

proposals.title: The Monolithic SubOS: A Performance Limit Study for Data-Centric Operating Systems
proposals.motivation: The prevailing trend in OS design, exemplified by the exokernel and microkernel lineages, is towards decomposition and specialization, with subOSes providing application-specific abstractions. This fragmentation, while offering flexibility, incurs overheads from inter-subOS communication and resource partitioning. It is critical to understand the fundamental performance trade-offs by examining the inverse architectural extreme.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a single, large, shared subOS, co-designed with a high-performance distributed trust substrate, can outperform a multi-subOS architecture for a broad class of data-centric datacenter applications by eliminating inter-process abstraction boundaries and enabling global optimization of resources like the network stack.
proposals.method: We will implement a monolithic subOS within a framework like the XOK/ExOS model or a unikernel base (e.g., IncludeOS), instrumented for fine-grained performance analysis. We will contrast it against a multi-subOS baseline running on a high-performance networking stack, informed by Shenker's prior work on Fast Networks and Data-Centric Networking. The evaluation will use a suite of representative datacenter workloads (e.g., key-value stores, data-parallel computations) to measure throughput, latency, and resource utilization under both architectures.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a crucial data point on the performance Pareto frontier for OS architectures, quantifying the potential cost of decomposition. It will yield design principles for when a unified abstraction layer is preferable and inform the future co-design of operating systems and high-speed networks.
proposals.novelty: While multi-subOS designs are actively researched, a rigorous experimental investigation of the monolithic subOS as a deliberate, performance-optimized counterpoint—rather than a legacy artifact—is absent. The study's novelty lies in framing monolithic design as a limit case for modern, network-optimized systems.

proposals.title: Distributed SubOS: A Resource-Aware Abstraction for Multi-Node Service Clusters
proposals.motivation: The subOS concept, as a minimal layer between applications and the kernel, provides a powerful abstraction for managing local resources. However, modern services are inherently distributed, running across clusters of machines. Current cluster orchestration systems (e.g., Kubernetes) operate at a high level, lacking the fine-grained, performance-isolated resource management that a subOS could provide across a networked pool of physical resources.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the subOS abstraction can be extended to span multiple physical nodes, creating a single, coherent, and secure distributed resource manager that provides applications with the illusion of a unified, richly-resourced machine while efficiently managing locality, communication, and failure.
proposals.method: We will design a distributed subOS architecture using a two-tier control plane. A global 'orchestrator' subOS will manage cluster-wide resource allocation and placement using a Paxos-variant for consensus, informed by a network-aware cost model. Local subOS instances on each node will enforce allocations and handle fast-path operations. We will implement a distributed capability system, extending prior work on high-performance distributed trust, to secure cross-node resource delegation. The system will be evaluated on a testbed using microservice and data-centric networking workloads.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a new primitive for distributed systems: a low-level, resource-centric abstraction for clustered hardware. It will demonstrate how to unify cluster scheduling and local isolation, potentially improving performance predictability for latency-sensitive, distributed applications. The design will also advance the integration of trust and security models into the foundational layers of resource management.
proposals.novelty: While cluster managers and single-node OS abstractions exist, fusing them into a single, minimal distributed subOS layer is novel. This approach moves the resource-aware 'spirit' of the subOS into the network, treating a cluster not as a set of cooperating machines but as a single, disaggregated computer from the application's perspective.

proposals.title: Consistency as a Service: Applying Database CRDTs to Information-Centric Network Caches
proposals.motivation: Information-Centric Networking (ICN) promises efficient content distribution through pervasive in-network caching, but its semantics for consistency of mutable content remain ad-hoc and poorly defined. This creates a fundamental barrier for deploying ICN as a general-purpose service platform for dynamic data. The problem mirrors challenges in distributed databases, where formal consistency models provide clear trade-offs between performance and correctness.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that formal consistency models from distributed database theory, specifically Conflict-Free Replicated Data Types (CRDTs) and explicit consistency levels (e.g., causal, eventual), can be systematically applied to ICN's data plane to provide predictable, tunable semantics for cached replicas, enabling new classes of distributed services.
proposals.method: We will design a 'Consistency-as-a-Service' layer atop an ICN architecture like NDN or CCNx. The core is a distributed protocol where content names are augmented with metadata encoding CRDT semantics or version vectors. We will implement a prototype extending the CICN/NFD forwarder to interpret this metadata, coordinating updates via a lightweight, epidemic anti-entropy process among caches, inspired by Dynamo-style databases. The evaluation will measure latency, bandwidth overhead, and application-perceived consistency violations for workloads like collaborative editing and IoT state updates.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first formal framework for reasoning about consistency in ICN, moving beyond simple cache invalidation. It will deliver a concrete, open-source prototype demonstrating how database principles can be integrated into the network layer, enabling ICN to natively support distributed, mutable-state applications with guaranteed semantics.
proposals.novelty: While prior ICN work acknowledges the consistency problem, our approach is novel in its direct translation of rigorous database consistency models and CRDTs into the network data plane itself, treating the cache network as a distributed storage system with programmable semantics.

proposals.title: The P4-Logic Interface: A Declarative Coordination Layer for Imperative Data Plane Programming
proposals.motivation: Modern network control and distributed systems coordination are increasingly complex. While declarative languages (e.g., Datalog variants, P4log) elegantly specify high-level policies and coordination logic, low-level packet processing demands the performance and precise control of imperative languages like P4. A clean, principled interface between these paradigms is missing, forcing developers to choose between expressiveness and efficiency.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a carefully designed, two-layer architecture—where a declarative logic engine handles coordination, consistency, and policy, and an imperative P4 data plane executes performance-critical packet operations—can achieve both high-level programmability and near-line-rate performance without sacrificing either.
proposals.method: We will design and implement a system where a centralized or distributed logic engine, inspired by prior work on NDlog and P4log, compiles declarative coordination rules (e.g., for membership, load balancing, or consensus) into two outputs: 1) imperative P4 code for in-network actions (e.g., packet forwarding, header manipulation, telemetry collection), and 2) control messages to install/update table entries. We will define a formal interface (API) between the logic engine and the P4 switch, specifying the mapping from logical predicates to table match-action rules. A prototype will be built on the BMv2/P4Runtime software switch framework and evaluated using microbenchmarks and a distributed service coordination case study.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel system architecture and a concrete implementation blueprint for hybrid declarative-imperative networking. It will demonstrate how to cleanly separate coordination logic from packet processing, advancing the vision of data-centric networking. The evaluation will provide quantitative evidence of the performance-expressiveness trade-offs inherent in this design.
proposals.novelty: While declarative networking and P4 are both mature pre-2026 fields, their integration has been ad-hoc. This proposal's novelty lies in formally defining and implementing a principled interface between a general-purpose declarative coordination layer and a fully programmable imperative data plane, moving beyond domain-specific compilers.

proposals.title: Declarative Asynchronous High-Latency (DAHL) Programming: A Language for Partition-Tolerant Distributed Services
proposals.motivation: Modern distributed services increasingly operate in challenging environments like global edge computing, satellite networks, and developing regions, where high latency, network partitions, and message loss are the norm, not the exception. Existing distributed programming languages and frameworks, designed for reliable datacenter networks, impose complex, error-prone synchronization logic on developers, making it difficult to build robust applications for these unreliable substrates.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a declarative, data-centric language, extending the principles of DAHL (Data-centric High-level Language) to explicitly model and embrace network asynchrony and unreliability, can significantly simplify the development of correct, efficient, and partition-tolerant distributed services.
proposals.method: We will design a new language by augmenting DAHL's core abstractions—data objects and declarative service statements—with explicit, programmer-controlled semantics for eventual consistency, conflict-free replicated data types (CRDTs), and probabilistic delivery guarantees. The compiler and runtime will be built on a novel 'intent propagation' substrate, inspired by data-centric networking principles, which separates service logic from the unreliable message-passing layer. This substrate will use epidemic protocols and version vectors to manage state convergence under partitions, allowing developers to reason about high-level data flow rather than low-level network failures.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a new programming model and language specification for unreliable networks, 2) a reference compiler and runtime implementation demonstrating its feasibility, and 3) a formal framework for reasoning about safety and liveness in declarative, asynchronous systems. It will advance the field of distributed systems by bridging high-level service design with the realities of non-ideal network environments.
proposals.novelty: While prior work on CRDTs and eventual consistency provides low-level building blocks, our approach is novel in embedding these concepts directly into a high-level, declarative language and compiler, shifting the burden of handling asynchrony from the programmer to the system's data-centric runtime.

proposals.title: DAHL in the Fire: A Comparative, Adversarial Evaluation of a Declarative Framework for Verifiable BFT Consensus
proposals.motivation: Building correct, high-performance Byzantine Fault-Tolerant (BFT) consensus systems remains a critical and arduous challenge. While formal verification frameworks and practical systems libraries exist, they often represent a trade-off between expressiveness, ease of verification, and runtime robustness. A new declarative framework like DAHL, which aims to unify specification and implementation, must be rigorously evaluated against this trade-off to demonstrate its practical value for building complex, trustworthy distributed services.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a BFT consensus protocol (e.g., PBFT) implemented in the DAHL framework will achieve comparable formal verifiability to dedicated specification languages like TLA+, while offering superior runtime robustness and debuggability under adversarial conditions compared to implementations in operational frameworks like LibP2P or Go.
proposals.method: We will implement a canonical BFT protocol (e.g., a simplified PBFT variant) in DAHL and, for baseline, in TLA+ (for formal modeling) and a practical framework like Go with gRPC/LibP2P. The DAHL specification will be formally verified for safety and liveness under partial synchrony. All implementations will be subjected to a controlled, adversarial test campaign in a simulated network (e.g., using Mininet or a custom event-driven simulator), systematically injecting partitions, message delays/drops, and Byzantine behaviors to measure performance metrics and failure modes.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first quantitative and qualitative evaluation of DAHL's end-to-end capability for a core distributed systems problem. It will yield a concrete analysis of the framework's strengths and weaknesses in verification expressiveness, implementation fidelity, and operational resilience, guiding its future development and adoption.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the holistic, adversarial comparison methodology that bridges the formal verification and practical systems communities, directly testing the claim that a declarative framework can effectively span this gap for a non-trivial protocol like BFT consensus.

proposals.title: Succession: A Bio-Inspired Framework for the Gradual Evolution of DAHL Systems
proposals.motivation: Building complex, reliable distributed systems remains a brittle and monolithic engineering challenge, especially in the context of Declarative, Adaptive, High-Level (DAHL) abstractions. Current approaches often require a complete, correct specification upfront, which is antithetical to the adaptive spirit of DAHL. This work addresses the core problem of how to robustly bootstrap sophisticated system properties (e.g., consensus, fault tolerance, load balancing) from simple, verifiable beginnings.
proposals.hypothesis: A distributed system can be architected to autonomously evolve through a sequence of increasingly complex and stable states, guided by general compositional rules analogous to ecological succession, thereby achieving greater robustness and adaptability than a monolithic design.
proposals.method: We will design a rule-based succession framework for DAHL. First, we will define a catalog of simple, composable 'pioneer' protocols (e.g., basic membership, heartbeat). Second, we will formalize 'succession rules'—declarative predicates that specify when a system state enables the safe introduction of a new component (e.g., a leader election protocol can be introduced once a stable quorum is detectable). The system will start with a minimal pioneer set and use a lightweight, DAHL-based controller to evaluate rules and orchestrate the staged integration of subsequent layers like consensus or sharding, each building upon the established prior state.
proposals.contribution: This research will yield a novel framework for constructing complex distributed systems via guided, automatic evolution, directly advancing the DAHL paradigm. It will provide concrete mechanisms to improve system resilience and adaptability, and a formal model linking ecological principles to distributed systems design.
proposals.novelty: While prior work uses biological metaphors (e.g., anti-entropy, epidemics), the direct application of ecological succession's staged, rule-based community development to system architecture is unexplored. This moves beyond composition to prescribed, state-dependent evolution.

proposals.title: Economic-Substance-Aware Distributed Systems: A DAHL Framework for Provable Compliance
proposals.motivation: Modern distributed platforms (e.g., for gig work, decentralized finance) face a critical gap: their technical correctness does not guarantee compliance with the economic and legal substance of the interactions they facilitate. Pre-2026, systems are verified against formal algorithmic specs, but these specs ignore the underlying economic reality—such as value flows and de facto employment relationships—leading to regulatory risk and systemic misalignment.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the Distributed Abstract Higher-Order Logic (DAHL) framework can be extended to formally specify and verify the economic-substance properties of a distributed system, enabling the construction of systems whose execution traces are provably correct with respect to both functional logic and intended socio-economic contracts.
proposals.method: We will extend the DAHL framework with a formal ontology for economic primitives (e.g., obligation, value transfer, control). System specifications will integrate these primitives with traditional distributed state machines. We will develop a model-checking and runtime verification layer that audits execution traces against economic-substance invariants, building upon prior work in distributed consensus and logic-based verification. A case study will implement a simplified gig-work matching service to demonstrate the prevention of de facto employment relationships by construction.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first formal framework for defining and enforcing economic-substance correctness in distributed systems. It will yield a new class of 'legally aware' system designs and a concrete toolchain for developers to achieve regulatory compliance by construction, bridging computer science with economic and legal modeling.
proposals.novelty: This inverts the standard verification focus from statutory algorithmic purpose to de facto economic effect, using a higher-order logic framework to bind technical execution to socio-economic constraints—a synthesis not present in pre-2026 distributed systems or formal methods literature.

proposals.title: DAHL as a Universal Substrate: Implementing and Evaluating Complex Distributed Services
proposals.motivation: The DAHL (Distributed Aggregatable Hierarchical Log) architecture, introduced in the Fast Networks for High-Performance Distributed Trust paper, demonstrated a novel, data-centric foundation for distributed systems. However, its initial evaluation was limited to three specific services (trust, consensus, coordination). The networking community lacks a clear understanding of whether DAHL's principles of hierarchical aggregation and immutable logs are broadly applicable to the complex, stateful services that form the backbone of modern cloud infrastructure.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the DAHL architecture can serve as a universal, high-performance substrate for implementing a wide class of complex distributed services, offering inherent advantages in debuggability, consistency, and performance over traditional RPC-based designs.
proposals.method: We will select and implement 2-3 canonical, complex distributed systems (e.g., a distributed key-value store with transactions, a stateful stream processing engine, or a sharded database layer) directly atop the core DAHL primitives. The implementation will rigorously adhere to DAHL's data-centric spirit, modeling all service state and operations as append-only logs and hierarchical aggregates. We will develop new aggregation functions and log-structured data models specific to each service's semantics. Performance and correctness will be evaluated against equivalent systems built with conventional RPC and consensus libraries (e.g., gRPC, Raft).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first comprehensive evidence for or against DAHL's generality, moving it from a promising prototype to a credible systems architecture. It will yield concrete design patterns for mapping complex service logic to DAHL's abstractions and identify the architectural limits where a data-centric approach excels or falters.
proposals.novelty: While prior work introduced DAHL, its application was narrow. This proposal systematically tests its core premise of universality by building services with complex, mutable state—a significant leap beyond the initially evaluated examples. The research applies a 'teaching' methodology of deep, principled implementation to validate a foundational architectural statement.

proposals.title: Optimizing Distributed Asynchronous Heterogeneous Learning (DAHL) Runtime Through Data-Centric Scheduling and Fast Network Primitives
proposals.motivation: Distributed Asynchronous Heterogeneous Learning (DAHL) frameworks are critical for modern machine learning workloads across diverse, non-uniform hardware. However, pre-2026, their runtime performance is often bottlenecked by inefficient task scheduling and high-latency communication, especially in high-performance distributed trust environments. Optimizing the DAHL runtime is essential to fully utilize heterogeneous clusters and reduce training time for large-scale models.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a data-centric scheduling policy, informed by real-time network performance and hardware capability telemetry, combined with low-latency network primitives for gradient and model exchange, will significantly reduce DAHL job completion time and improve cluster utilization.
proposals.method: We will instrument the DAHL runtime to collect fine-grained telemetry on task execution times, data locality, and network latency. We will design and implement a new scheduler that uses this data to place computational tasks, prioritizing data locality and minimizing stragglers. This scheduler will be integrated with fast network primitives, inspired by RDMA and in-network aggregation techniques, to optimize the communication phase. Performance will be evaluated using benchmark ML workloads on a heterogeneous testbed.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a production-ready, optimized DAHL runtime with a novel scheduling algorithm. It will provide a quantitative analysis of the performance gains from co-designing scheduling with network primitives in heterogeneous environments, advancing the state-of-the-art in distributed ML systems.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the tight integration of a data- and network-aware scheduler within the DAHL paradigm, moving beyond static placement to dynamic, performance-informed scheduling. This approach treats the network as a first-class, measurable resource for distributed learning, a perspective emphasized in data-centric networking but not fully realized in DAHL systems pre-2026.

proposals.title: Declarative Debugging: A Data-Centric Approach to Observing and Diagnosing DAHL Programs
proposals.motivation: Distributed systems are notoriously difficult to debug due to non-deterministic execution and complex state interactions. The emerging Declarative Application Hosting Layer (DAHL) abstracts resource management but, as a nascent system, lacks the debugging tools necessary for practical adoption. Without specialized observability, developers cannot effectively reason about the behavior of their distributed applications, hindering both development and deployment.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a debugging framework built on DAHL's core data-centric and declarative principles can expose causal relationships and state evolution in a way that is more comprehensible than traditional log-based or event-tracing tools for distributed systems.
proposals.method: We will design a debugging service that instruments the DAHL runtime to capture a provenance graph of data dependencies and state transitions, as declared by the program's logic. This service will integrate with a visualization front-end to render these graphs interactively, allowing developers to query the system's history (e.g., "why did this value appear?") and simulate hypothetical executions. The core algorithm will adapt causal logging and lineage tracking techniques, but applied to the declarative statements of DAHL rather than low-level message events.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver the first open-source, integrated debugging suite for DAHL, lowering the barrier to building reliable distributed services. It will contribute a novel model for debugging that elevates the abstraction level from network messages to programmatic intent, advancing the field of distributed system observability.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior distributed debuggers that trace messages or function calls, our tool is built from the ground up for a declarative, data-centric programming model, making the system's logical dependencies the primary unit of observation and interrogation.

proposals.title: DAHL-WAN: A Declarative, Hierarchical Framework for Internet-Scale Distributed Systems
proposals.motivation: Building and managing large-scale, wide-area distributed systems across administrative domains remains a complex, error-prone task, requiring intricate handling of consistency, fault tolerance, and performance. Existing frameworks often impose rigid programming models or lack the scalability for thousands of heterogeneous nodes. A declarative, high-level approach could simplify this process, but its feasibility at internet scale is unproven.
proposals.hypothesis: The Declarative Adaptive Hierarchical Language (DAHL) model, which separates policy (what) from mechanism (how), can be successfully adapted to coordinate thousands of nodes across the wide area, providing a simpler, more robust foundation for internet-scale services than imperative, tightly-coupled designs.
proposals.method: We will extend the DAHL architecture with a scalable, hierarchical coordination service inspired by recent work on high-performance distributed trust. The core technical approach involves: 1) Designing a new 'place' abstraction for wide-area administrative domains, enabling policy delegation and trust management. 2) Developing a gossip-based dissemination layer for scalable state propagation and membership, moving beyond single-cluster assumptions. 3) Creating a compiler that translates high-level service specifications into fault-tolerant execution plans spanning these hierarchical 'places'.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a practical, open-source framework for building internet-scale systems declaratively. It will provide concrete evidence on the performance and manageability trade-offs of the DAHL model at scale, contributing a new architectural blueprint for the next generation of wide-area distributed services.
proposals.novelty: While DAHL and other declarative systems (e.g., P2) have been explored in data-center contexts, applying and adapting this 'data-centric' philosophy to the heterogeneous, trust-partitioned environment of the global internet is a significant and unexplored leap. The integration of hierarchical trust domains as a first-class primitive within the declarative model is a key innovation.

proposals.title: Dual: A Centralized Simulator for Rapid Prototyping and Verification of Distributed DAHL Programs
proposals.motivation: Distributed Algorithmic High-level Language (DAHL) frameworks promise to simplify the creation of correct, efficient distributed systems by abstracting low-level coordination. However, pre-2026, developing and debugging these systems remains challenging due to the inherent non-determinism and scale of distributed execution. A fast, centralized simulation engine is needed to allow developers to rapidly iterate on DAHL program logic, test correctness under various network conditions, and predict performance before costly distributed deployment.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a centralized simulator, by faithfully modeling the logical execution and message-passing semantics of DAHL programs, can accurately predict the emergent behavior, convergence properties, and performance bottlenecks of its distributed counterpart, serving as a powerful pre-deployment verification tool.
proposals.method: We will design Dual as a discrete-event simulation engine that executes DAHL intermediate representation (IR). It will maintain a global virtual clock and simulate concurrent logical processes (LPs) representing nodes. The core will be a scheduler that serializes events (e.g., message delivery, timer expiry) deterministically based on modeled network delays and failures. We will implement specific models for packet loss, variable latency, and partition faults, drawing from prior work in network simulation (e.g., ns-3 principles). The simulator will be instrumented to output detailed traces for behavioral analysis and performance prediction.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first dedicated simulation and debugging environment for the DAHL ecosystem, significantly lowering the barrier to developing reliable distributed algorithms. It will contribute a methodology for 'shift-left' verification in distributed systems, enabling rigorous testing during the design phase. The simulator itself will become a core tool for both research and teaching in distributed computing.
proposals.novelty: While distributed simulators exist, a centralized simulator specifically architected to execute the novel abstraction of DAHL's IR for the purpose of developer-in-the-loop prototyping and behavioral prediction is a new approach. It inverts the typical relationship by using a centralized tool to *teach* and verify the *spirit* of a distributed system's logic before it runs in a *distributed* setting.

proposals.title: DAHL for Hybrid Scale: A Declarative Framework for Managing Application Complexity Across Cloud and Edge
proposals.motivation: The proliferation of heterogeneous cloud and edge computing resources presents a fundamental management challenge. Current orchestration systems (e.g., Kubernetes) require imperative, low-level specifications that are brittle and do not scale in complexity, making it difficult to express and enforce high-level application intent across diverse, geographically dispersed resources. This limits the ability to deploy intelligent, latency-sensitive, and data-intensive applications at the edge efficiently.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the declarative, intent-based abstraction of the Distributed Application Hierarchy Language (DAHL) can be extended to manage the inherent scale and heterogeneity of hybrid cloud/edge environments, simplifying application specification while enabling the system to autonomously handle complex placement and lifecycle decisions.
proposals.method: We will extend the DAHL model with new resource and constraint primitives for edge-specific properties (e.g., location, intermittent connectivity, hardware accelerators). A core technical contribution will be a two-tiered solver architecture: a high-level intent compiler that generates a constraint satisfaction problem, and a distributed, incremental constraint solver that integrates with existing cluster schedulers (like Kubernetes) to make near-optimal placement and scaling decisions. We will implement a prototype and evaluate it using trace-driven simulations and testbed deployments of representative microservice and stateful dataflow applications.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a formally grounded, declarative framework for hybrid cloud/edge application management that reduces specification complexity. It will provide a practical system demonstrating how high-level intent can be translated into robust distributed orchestration, advancing the state of data-centric and service-oriented networking.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in applying DAHL's principled declarative logic—previously explored in narrower contexts—to the unruly, large-scale heterogeneity of the edge, moving beyond simple placement to manage dynamic adaptation and complex service dependencies declaratively.

proposals.title: The Network as the Bottleneck: A Data-Centric Analysis of Pre-Server Performance Degradation
proposals.motivation: Modern distributed systems and data center designs overwhelmingly focus on server-side resource bottlenecks (CPU, memory, storage). However, the rise of disaggregated architectures, microservices, and high-performance distributed trust mechanisms (e.g., consensus protocols) places immense, complex load on the network fabric before requests even reach a server's compute resources. This pre-server network path, encompassing load balancers, network stacks, and internal data movement, is a critical but under-instrumented and under-modeled component of end-to-end latency and throughput.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that for a broad class of modern data-centric services, the dominant source of performance degradation and tail latency is not server resource exhaustion, but contention and inefficiency in the network-level processing stages that precede server execution.
proposals.method: We will develop a measurement framework to decompose request service time into distinct pre-server (network fabric, load balancer queueing, kernel networking stack) and server-side phases. Building on Shenker's data-centric networking principles, we will instrument the network data plane (using P4) and host stack to trace request fate-sharing. We will then design and evaluate 'In-Network Load Shedding'—a mechanism where programmable switches, using lightweight ML classifiers trained on request headers, can intelligently drop or reroute requests predicted to cause downstream congestion before they consume server resources, preserving service health for high-priority traffic.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first comprehensive taxonomy and quantitative model of pre-server bottlenecks in data-centric networks. It will contribute a novel, practical architecture for network-assisted load management that moves bottleneck mitigation upstream, fundamentally shifting the design point for high-performance services.
proposals.novelty: While prior work optimizes server efficiency or network throughput, our approach uniquely treats the pre-server network path as a first-class, manageable resource bottleneck and proposes active, in-network decision-making to preserve it, a departure from reactive server-centric control loops.

proposals.title: Generalized Speak-Up: A Multi-Resource Defense Against Application-Layer DDoS Attacks
proposals.motivation: Distributed systems are vulnerable to low-rate application-layer DDoS attacks that target specific, non-CPU resources like memory, database connections, or I/O queues, creating bottlenecks that degrade service. Existing defenses like Speak-Up are effective for CPU-bound attacks but fail when the bottleneck shifts, leaving services unprotected against a broader class of strategic attacks. This gap is critical as modern microservices and data-centric architectures expose diverse resource constraints.
