proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the core Speak-Up principle—forcing clients to expend more of the bottlenecked resource than the attacker can provision—can be generalized into a unified control framework that dynamically identifies the contested resource and applies a tailored, resource-specific work multiplier.
proposals.method: We will design a Multi-Resource Speak-Up (MR-Speak) architecture. It will extend the original system with lightweight per-resource sensors (e.g., memory allocator hooks, connection pool monitors) to detect bottleneck shifts. A central controller will use a decision function to select the dominant bottleneck and activate a corresponding work-generation module (e.g., memory-intensive dummy allocations, connection-holding tasks). The client puzzle mechanism will be adapted to demand work that stresses the identified resource, preserving the system's distributed and incentive-compatible spirit.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first principled defense against multi-resource application-layer DDoS attacks, significantly expanding the robustness of distributed services. It will contribute a novel control framework for dynamic resource bottleneck identification and mitigation, advancing the field beyond single-resource defenses.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in moving from a static, CPU-centric defense to a dynamic, resource-agnostic control system that can adapt to shifting attack targets, a non-trivial generalization requiring new sensing and resource-specific work-generation mechanisms.

proposals.title: Server-Enforced Speak-Up: Incentivizing Client Participation in Distributed Trust Without Client Modification
proposals.motivation: Distributed trust and coordination systems like 'Speak-Up' rely on clients voluntarily adjusting request rates to signal trustworthiness, a model that fails with legacy or non-cooperative clients. In modern data-centric networks, servers must manage load and incentivize honest behavior from a heterogeneous mix of participants, including those running fixed, proprietary, or malicious software. This creates a critical performance and fairness gap in high-performance distributed systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a server can unilaterally enforce a 'speak-up'-like equilibrium by strategically modulating its own response latency and bandwidth allocation based on inferred client intent, thereby incentivizing desired client behavior without requiring client-side modifications.
proposals.method: We will design a server-side control algorithm that classifies incoming request streams (e.g., via request patterns and timing) into cooperative or non-cooperative cohorts. For non-cooperative streams, the server will apply a dynamic response shaping policy, using techniques from AQM (Active Queue Management) and virtual-credit systems. The policy will artificially increase latency or probabilistically drop requests for clients deemed to be 'quiet' (low-rate but potentially selfish), while maintaining high-quality service for clients whose aggregate request rate aligns with a system-wide fairness target derived from distributed trust principles.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a practical, incrementally deployable mechanism for bringing the benefits of cooperative protocols to non-cooperative environments. It will contribute a formal model and implementation of server-enforced incentive compatibility for distributed systems, extending the 'data-centric' and 'distributed trust' research themes. The resulting system will be evaluated on a testbed to demonstrate improved aggregate throughput and fairness in mixed-client scenarios.
proposals.novelty: Existing work places the burden of adaptation on clients; our approach is novel in shifting the enforcement mechanism entirely to the server side, using observable behavior to induce cooperation. This inverts the standard model of protocol deployment and integrates network control theory with economic incentive design.

proposals.title: Distributed Trust at the Edge: A Scalable, Data-Centric Defense for Content Delivery Networks
proposals.motivation: The increasing centralization of trust in large-scale CDNs and cloud-edge deployments creates critical performance bottlenecks and single points of failure for security protocols. Pre-2026 trends show a surge in DDoS attacks and sophisticated data-poisoning threats targeting these distributed infrastructures, demanding defenses that are both high-performance and inherently decentralized. Existing trust mechanisms often fail to scale efficiently across thousands of edge nodes while maintaining low latency for content delivery.
proposals.hypothesis: By implementing a data-centric, lightweight trust consensus protocol directly within the forwarding plane of CDN/edge nodes, we can achieve scalable, attack-resilient content delivery without relying on centralized coordination or sacrificing performance.
proposals.method: We will design and implement a novel protocol, building on concepts from Shenker's prior work on high-performance distributed trust and data-centric networking. The approach will embed verifiable attestations of content integrity and origin within the network layer, using efficient cryptographic primitives like BLS signatures for aggregation. A gossip-based consensus mechanism among edge nodes will propagate trust statements, enabling rapid, localized validation of content requests and responses without a global coordinator. We will prototype this system within a programmable data plane (e.g., P4) and evaluate it on a testbed simulating a large CDN topology.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a practical architecture for scalable, trust-aware content delivery, directly addressing the performance-security trade-off in modern CDNs. It will provide a concrete instantiation of data-centric networking principles for security, advancing the field beyond theoretical models. The resulting open-source prototype and performance analysis will serve as a benchmark for future distributed trust systems.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the tight integration of a distributed trust consensus mechanism into the data plane of a CDN, moving trust from an overlay service to an intrinsic property of the content delivery path itself. This departs from traditional, heavier-weight application-layer trust orchestration.

proposals.title: Protocols as Learned Services: A Data-Centric Framework for Adaptive Network Stacks
proposals.motivation: Network protocols are static documents, designed for worst-case assumptions and frozen at deployment. This rigidity is at odds with the dynamic, data-rich environments of modern data centers and distributed systems, where performance and trust requirements evolve. A fixed protocol cannot efficiently adapt to observed traffic patterns, application demands, or emerging trust models, leading to persistent inefficiency.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that core protocol logic—including flow control, congestion management, and trust negotiation—can be modeled as a learned service, where parameters and even decision functions are continuously and efficiently adapted based on interim operational data without breaking interoperability.
proposals.method: We will develop a 'protocol compiler' that separates a protocol's invariant skeleton (e.g., packet formats, handshake sequences) from its adaptive policy core. The policy core will be implemented as a set of lightweight, retrainable models (e.g., decision trees, small neural networks) updated via online learning from telemetry data. We will instantiate this framework by redesigning a core protocol (e.g., BBR or a distributed trust negotiation primitive) as a case study, building on Shenker's prior work on data-centric networking and high-performance distributed trust.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a formal model and architecture for incrementally deployable, adaptive protocols, 2) a concrete implementation and evaluation of an adaptive congestion control or trust protocol demonstrating significant performance gains, and 3) a pedagogical framework (extending the 'Teaching Statement' spirit) for rethinking protocol design education.
proposals.novelty: While prior work applies machine learning to optimize protocol parameters, our approach inverts the model: the protocol itself is a service that learns and evolves, fundamentally challenging the notion of a protocol as a static specification. This integrates the 'data-centric' and 'learning' themes into the core of distributed systems architecture.

proposals.title: Distributed Consensus on Protocol Evolution: A Git-Inspired Framework for Transparent Amendment Tracking
proposals.motivation: The development and maintenance of network protocols and distributed system specifications are collaborative, asynchronous processes prone to ambiguity. Current practices for tracking changes, amendments, and approvals (e.g., email threads, static RFCs) lack the transparency, auditability, and structured collaboration tools that modern software development enjoys via systems like Git. This opacity hinders the verification of protocol properties, complicates the teaching of protocol evolution, and impedes the establishment of trust in the resulting standards, especially for high-performance distributed systems.
proposals.hypothesis: Applying the core distributed version control and collaboration model of Git to protocol documents will create a transparent, verifiable, and efficient system for managing protocol evolution, thereby increasing trust and reducing errors in distributed system implementations.
proposals.method: We will design a specialized data model for protocol specifications, treating sections, formal statements (e.g., TLA+, P4 code), and normative text as versioned objects. We will adapt Git's Merkle DAG and branching model to support structured amendment proposals, formal reviews, and merge conflicts resolution specific to protocol semantics. The system will be implemented as a decentralized service, leveraging conflict-free replicated data types (CRDTs) or a Byzantine-tolerant consensus layer (inspired by Shenker's work on distributed trust) to manage concurrent edits and approvals without a central authority. A web-based interface will visualize the evolution graph and diff semantic constructs, not just text.
proposals.contribution: This work will yield a novel, open-source framework for protocol development that provides an immutable, auditable history of changes and decisions. It will contribute a formal model for protocol evolution as a distributed state machine, enhancing the field's methodology for collaborative specification. The tool itself will serve as a pedagogical asset for teaching the lifecycle of distributed systems.
proposals.novelty: While version control is standard for code, its principled application to the *process* of protocol standardization—integrating distributed consensus for amendment approval and providing semantic diffs for formal specifications—is a novel, data-centric approach to networking research.

proposals.title: Dynamic Proximate Trust: Enabling Fluid Membership in Distributed Byzantine-Prone Systems
proposals.motivation: Distributed Byzantine-Prone Trust (DBPT) systems, as explored in recent work on high-performance distributed trust, are designed for fixed, known participants. In real-world deployments, such as federated learning consortia or dynamic service meshes, participants need to join or leave the trust domain without halting computation. Pre-2026 systems lack efficient, secure protocols for such fluid membership, limiting the applicability of strong trust guarantees to static scenarios.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a DBPT system can be extended with a lightweight, consensus-driven reconfiguration protocol that allows dynamic membership changes while preserving the system's safety and liveness properties and maintaining high performance for the core computation.
proposals.method: We will design a two-layer architecture: a core DBPT execution layer (e.g., based on modern BFT state machine replication) and a separate reconfiguration consensus group. The reconfiguration protocol will use verifiable state proofs (like authenticated system snapshots) to allow new members to bootstrap trust efficiently. We will adapt proactive secret sharing and threshold cryptosystem refresh techniques from pre-2026 literature to securely redistribute trust credentials when members depart, ensuring the proximate trust domain's integrity.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first formal model and practical implementation for dynamic membership in DBPT systems. It will extend the principles of 'data-centric' and 'service'-oriented networking to trust management, enabling long-lived, adaptable trustworthy computations. The resulting protocols will be evaluated in a testbed to quantify the overhead of membership changes.
proposals.novelty: While BFT reconfiguration exists, applying it to the specific 'proximate trust' model—where trust is derived from ongoing participation and performance—and integrating it seamlessly with high-throughput data-centric computation is a novel challenge. The solution lies in the careful decoupling of reconfiguration logic from service logic, a 'spirit' aligned with clean-slate network design.

proposals.title: Distributed Byzantine Fault-Tolerant LANs over Mutually Distrustful Infrastructure Providers
proposals.motivation: The rise of multi-tenant data centers and edge computing clusters necessitates shared LANs where the physical network infrastructure is operated by multiple, potentially adversarial, entities. Pre-2026 trust models for distributed systems (like BFT state machine replication) assume a trusted network layer, which is unrealistic in this setting. This gap prevents secure, high-performance distributed services (e.g., coordination, storage) from being deployed across administrative domains.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a LAN abstraction can be constructed atop mutually distrustful network providers using a combination of cryptographic verification and redundant path transmission, such that the logical network appears reliable and consistent to endpoints even if a subset of providers is Byzantine.
proposals.method: We will design and implement DBPT-LAN, extending the Dataplane BFT (DBFT) principles from 'Fast Networks for High-Performance Distributed Trust' to the physical infrastructure layer. The core method involves endpoints sending packet copies across disjoint paths managed by different providers, with providers generating succinct, verifiable proofs of packet forwarding (e.g., using Merkle trees of packet digests). A lightweight consensus protocol among endpoints, not providers, will agree on the correct packet sequence, filtering out inconsistencies introduced by malicious infrastructure.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first formal model and practical system for a Byzantine fault-tolerant LAN service without a trusted network operator. It will contribute a new architecture for secure multi-provider network services and a set of protocols that separate logical trust (endpoints) from physical infrastructure control. This directly enables new distributed applications in untrusted colocation environments.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior BFT systems that trust the network, or secure routing protocols that assume some trusted core, our approach treats the entire physical forwarding fabric as a Byzantine entity, a significantly stronger adversarial model. The novelty lies in applying a 'data-centric' verification framework to physical network infrastructure itself.

proposals.title: Asynchronous Trust for Intermittent Participants in Distributed Systems
proposals.motivation: Distributed trust and consensus protocols, such as those in blockchains and federated learning, typically assume synchronous or semi-synchronous networks where participants are mostly online. This assumption fails in real-world edge and mobile environments where devices have intermittent connectivity, high latency, or operate offline for extended periods. Enabling secure, verifiable participation for these asynchronous agents is crucial for expanding the applicability of distributed systems to a broader, more realistic set of scenarios.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by decoupling the act of making a statement (e.g., a transaction, a model update) from the real-time consensus process, and by using cryptographic accumulators and verifiable delay functions, we can construct a trust layer that securely incorporates contributions from intermittently connected participants without compromising system integrity.
proposals.method: We will design a new protocol architecture inspired by Shenker's work on data-centric networking and trust. The core mechanism will involve participants cryptographically committing their statements to a local, verifiable log when offline. Upon reconnection, they present these logs with succinct proofs of correctness. A core set of online validators, using a modified BFT consensus, will asynchronously verify and batch these proofs into a persistent, global state using a Merkle forest accumulator. We will prototype this system within a framework like Hyperledger Fabric or a custom Rust-based testbed to evaluate latency, throughput, and security under realistic churn patterns.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a formal model and a practical protocol for asynchronous participation in distributed trust systems. It will extend the design space for consensus algorithms beyond traditional models, enabling new applications in mobile federated learning, IoT data markets, and delay-tolerant decentralized applications. The evaluation will provide concrete performance trade-offs for this new trust paradigm.
proposals.novelty: While asynchronous consensus exists, our approach uniquely focuses on the trust layer for *clients* or *edge participants* who are not part of the core validating committee, allowing them to make verifiable, state-altering contributions entirely offline. This blends data-centric commitment with distributed trust in a novel way not addressed by prior BFT or blockchain protocols.

proposals.title: Decentralized, Trust-Aware Federated Learning for Data-Centric DBPT LANs
proposals.motivation: The DBPT (Data-Centric Networking) architecture, which treats data as a first-class entity, is a promising substrate for collaborative AI within enterprise or campus LANs. However, existing distributed ML frameworks like Federated Learning (FL) are designed for client-server models over WANs, ignoring the unique low-latency, high-throughput, and inherent trust characteristics of a DBPT LAN. This mismatch leads to suboptimal resource utilization and fails to leverage the network's potential for secure, peer-to-peer model collaboration.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by co-designing the ML training and inference protocols with the DBPT architecture's data-centric primitives and local trust model, we can achieve significantly faster convergence, lower communication overhead, and more robust collaborative learning than adapting existing FL frameworks.
proposals.method: We will develop a new protocol, DBPT-FL, that replaces the central aggregator with a decentralized agreement protocol inspired by Shenker's work on distributed trust. Model updates (gradients or parameters) will be published as named data objects within the DBPT fabric. A gossip-based consensus mechanism, weighted by locally computed trust scores derived from update quality and node reliability, will govern the aggregation of these objects. We will implement and evaluate DBPT-FL using PyTorch and a DBPT simulator, comparing its performance against standard FedAvg on metrics of training time, accuracy, and resilience to Byzantine participants.
proposals.contribution: This work will yield a concrete, open-source framework for efficient collaborative AI on DBPT networks. It will contribute new design principles for integrating learning systems with data-centric networking and provide empirical evidence on the performance benefits of this co-design. The resulting trust-aware aggregation protocol is a contribution to robust distributed learning.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the synthesis of federated learning with a data-centric network architecture (DBPT) and a decentralized trust mechanism, moving beyond the simple adaptation of FL to a fundamental re-architecture tailored for a high-performance, trusted local network environment.

proposals.title: Cerberus: A Hardware Architecture for Proximate, Multi-Domain Trusted Execution
proposals.motivation: Distributed systems increasingly rely on Trusted Execution Environments (TEEs) like Intel SGX for secure multi-party computation. However, existing TEEs are designed for a single, monolithic trust domain, creating performance and security bottlenecks when multiple mutually distrusting parties need to co-locate computations on the same physical hardware. This is a critical limitation for emerging data-centric applications in cloud, edge, and financial settings where proximate but distinct trust domains must interact with low latency and high assurance.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a hardware architecture explicitly designed for multiple, isolated trust domains on a single chip can provide stronger security guarantees and higher performance for distributed, multi-party applications than software abstractions layered atop monolithic TEEs.
proposals.method: We will design a new secure hardware enclave architecture, Cerberus, with physically partitioned and cryptographically isolated execution units, memory, and I/O channels. The design will incorporate a hardware-rooted, distributed attestation protocol inspired by recent work on distributed trust, enabling efficient proof of integrity across domains. We will develop a formal model of the security properties and simulate the architecture using modified RISC-V or gem5 models to evaluate performance against SGX-based baselines for workloads like secure multi-party inference and distributed ledger operations.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a novel hardware blueprint for multi-domain TEEs, 2) a formal security model for proximate trust domains, and 3) quantitative evidence that hardware specialization can overcome the performance overheads of software-based isolation for distributed, data-centric tasks. It advances the vision of data-centric networking by moving trust primitives into the hardware fabric.
proposals.novelty: While prior work focuses on securing a single enclave or building distributed trust in software, Cerberus is novel in its first-principles hardware co-design for multiple, mutually distrusting entities on a single socket, moving beyond the 'one-enclave-per-chip' paradigm of pre-2026 TEEs.

proposals.title: Collective Cryptography: Applying HPC Communication Primitives to Distributed Trust Operations
proposals.motivation: Distributed trust systems, such as Byzantine consensus and threshold signature schemes, are fundamental to modern decentralized services but suffer from significant communication overhead. This bottleneck limits scalability and performance, especially in large, geographically distributed networks. Pre-2026, the networking community has focused on protocol design, while the HPC community has decades of expertise in optimizing collective operations for large-scale scientific computation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the structured communication patterns and topology-aware algorithms developed for HPC collectives (e.g., reduction, broadcast, all-gather) can be systematically adapted to dramatically reduce the latency and bandwidth consumption of cryptographic operations in distributed trust protocols.
proposals.method: We will map core cryptographic operations (e.g., multi-party computation of signatures, distributed randomness generation, consensus voting) onto HPC collective abstractions. We will implement and evaluate adaptations of specific algorithms like binomial tree broadcast, recursive doubling for reduction, and hypercube-based all-reduce within a prototype distributed trust framework. The evaluation will measure performance against baseline gossip or naive all-to-all communication in simulated and real-world network topologies, focusing on metrics like completion time and message complexity.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a new design framework for building high-performance distributed trust systems, grounded in proven HPC principles. It will yield concrete algorithmic improvements and open-source implementations that demonstrate order-of-magnitude performance gains for cryptographic collectives in wide-area networks.
proposals.novelty: While HPC collectives and distributed cryptography are both mature fields, their intersection is largely unexplored. The novelty lies in the formal cross-pollination: treating cryptographic primitives as communication-intensive compute kernels and applying decades of HPC optimization wisdom to them in a networked service context.

proposals.title: Co-Designed Cryptographic Primitives for High-Performance Distributed Trust
proposals.motivation: The pursuit of high-performance distributed trust, as in recent work on fast consensus and Byzantine fault tolerance, is fundamentally bottlenecked by the cost of cryptographic operations like digital signatures and verifications. Current software libraries and general-purpose CPUs impose severe latency and throughput limits, preventing these protocols from achieving the microsecond-scale latencies required for next-generation data-centric networks and services.
proposals.hypothesis: By co-designing cryptographic protocols with the capabilities of modern programmable network interface cards (SmartNICs) and data-plane programmable switches (e.g., P4), we can offload and accelerate core trust primitives, enabling distributed trust protocols to operate at line rate with minimal host CPU involvement.
proposals.method: We will first decompose protocols like PBFT or a modern variant into their core cryptographic steps (signature generation/aggregation/verification, hash chains). We will then design new protocol variants where these steps are mapped to a heterogeneous compute fabric: the switch data plane will handle fast, simple aggregations and integrity checks; the SmartNIC will manage per-session key material and perform asymmetric crypto operations; and the host will only handle complex logic and recovery. We will implement these co-designed primitives using the P4 language for switches and FPGA/Domain-Specific Architecture (DSA) programming for NICs, integrating them into a modified version of a high-performance distributed system framework.
proposals.contribution: This work will yield (1) a blueprint for architecting distributed trust protocols across NIC-switch-host hierarchies, (2) a concrete, open-source implementation demonstrating order-of-magnitude improvements in latency and throughput for consensus, and (3) a set of principles for a data-centric networking stack where trust is a native, hardware-accelerated service.
proposals.novelty: While hardware acceleration for crypto is known, the novelty lies in the holistic, cross-layer co-design of the *protocol logic itself* to exploit the specific strengths and placement of emerging programmable hardware, moving beyond simple offload to a truly integrated trust substrate.

proposals.title: Formally Verified Security for Distributed Byzantine Protocol Templates (DBPTs)
proposals.motivation: The design of secure distributed systems, especially those tolerating Byzantine faults, is notoriously error-prone. Recent work on Distributed Byzantine Protocol Templates (DBPTs) aims to provide reusable, high-performance building blocks for trust, but their security guarantees remain asserted rather than proven. As these protocols become foundational to critical infrastructure, formal verification is essential to prevent catastrophic failures.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that applying a combination of model checking and interactive theorem proving to the specification of DBPTs will reveal subtle design flaws and, upon refinement, produce machine-checkable proofs of core security properties like safety and liveness under Byzantine conditions.
proposals.method: We will first develop a formal specification of a key DBPT, such as a high-performance consensus core, in the TLA+ language for model checking with TLC. This will exhaustively explore state spaces to find violations. Subsequently, we will encode the refined specification and its desired properties (e.g., agreement, validity) in the Coq proof assistant. We will construct mechanized proofs, leveraging existing libraries for distributed reasoning, to verify the protocol's correctness against its abstract system model.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver the first formally verified security proofs for a class of high-performance DBPTs, setting a new standard for rigor in distributed systems research. It will also produce a reusable verification framework and a set of annotated, corrected protocol specifications that can be confidently used by practitioners.
proposals.novelty: While formal methods have been applied to classical consensus algorithms, their application to the novel, performance-optimized DBPT architectures proposed in recent 'Fast Networks' research is unexplored. The novelty lies in bridging the gap between these pragmatic, high-speed protocol templates and the rigorous guarantees of mechanized proof.

proposals.title: Minimizing Distributed Byzantine-Prone Time (DBPT) for Secure Two-Party Edge Collaboration
proposals.motivation: Edge computing promises low-latency services but requires resource-constrained devices to collaborate securely. Existing trust models, like those in data centers, are too heavy for these environments, leaving a gap for efficient, secure collaboration. The concept of 'proximate trust'—where trust is derived from physical or network proximity—offers a promising but unexplored foundation for lightweight security in edge pairings.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a formal model of proximate trust, implemented via a lightweight continuous attestation protocol, can minimize the Distributed Byzantine-Prone Time (DBPT)—the window of vulnerability to Byzantine faults—for two-party edge computations without imposing prohibitive overhead.
proposals.method: We will design a protocol where two edge devices establish a trust anchor via physical proximity (e.g., a short-range, one-time QR code exchange). This anchor seeds a continuous, low-overhead mutual attestation loop using efficient hash chains and heartbeat messages to verify liveness and correct state. We will adapt and simplify consensus primitives from Shenker's prior work on high-performance distributed trust to create a two-party 'trust maintenance' state machine, rigorously modeling and measuring its impact on DBPT.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a formal metric (DBPT) for evaluating security in ephemeral edge collaborations and a practical, minimalist protocol for achieving it. It will provide a blueprint for secure, ad-hoc edge services that are both performant and trustworthy, advancing the vision of data-centric, decentralized networking.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in applying and formalizing the 'proximate trust' concept specifically for the two-party edge case with a focus on minimizing vulnerability windows (DBPT), a departure from heavyweight, group-based Byzantine Fault Tolerance (BFT) systems common in data centers.

proposals.title: Inverse: A Data-Centric Architecture for High-Latency Tolerant Secure Multi-Party Computation
proposals.motivation: Secure Multi-Party Computation (MPC) enables collaborative analysis on private data, but its performance is severely degraded by high WAN latency, limiting its practical use for geographically distributed participants. Existing approaches focus on minimizing communication rounds, which is insufficient when network delays are fundamental, as in cross-continental or censorship-circumventing deployments. A new architectural principle is needed to make MPC performant *despite* high latency, not by fighting it.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by adopting a data-centric networking philosophy—decoupling computation from synchronous communication and treating intermediate states as named data objects—we can design MPC protocols where high latency primarily impacts initialization, not sustained throughput.
proposals.method: We will design the Inverse protocol suite, building on pre-2026 asynchronous MPC foundations and verifiable secret sharing. The core innovation is a 'computation scheduling' layer that models an MPC circuit as a DAG of states. Participants pre-commit inputs and then, asynchronously, pull named state objects from a distributed, immutable log (inspired by Certificate Transparency logs) to perform local computation steps, posting results back. A small set of 'verifier' nodes, using succinct proofs, can audit correctness without re-executing the entire computation, ensuring security in this loosely synchronized model.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a novel, latency-tolerant architecture for MPC that inverts the traditional synchronous communication model, 2) a concrete protocol specification and security analysis, and 3) an open-source implementation and evaluation demonstrating orders-of-magnitude better throughput over high-latency links compared to state-of-the-art synchronous MPC frameworks.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in explicitly designing for high-latency environments as a first-class constraint, applying data-centric and asynchronous systems principles—areas of Shenker's expertise—to the core design of cryptographic protocols, rather than treating latency as an external nuisance to be minimized.

proposals.title: Dual: A Data-Centric Framework for Optimal Security-Performance Trade-offs in LANs
proposals.motivation: Modern data centers and enterprise LANs must simultaneously support high-performance distributed applications and stringent security requirements, such as those for distributed trust and confidential computation. Current approaches treat performance and security as independent constraints, leading to either over-provisioned networks or suboptimal security. A principled framework to maximize security guarantees under a fixed, realistic LAN performance budget is critically needed.
proposals.hypothesis: By formulating the LAN security-performance trade-off as a constrained optimization problem with a data-centric abstraction, we can systematically derive configurations and protocols that provably maximize security (e.g., integrity, confidentiality, trust complexity) for any given performance envelope.
proposals.method: We will develop 'Dual', a framework that models the LAN as a data-flow graph where vertices are trust domains and edges are performance-constrained links. Security primitives (e.g., zero-knowledge proofs, homomorphic encryption, trusted execution environments) are modeled as computational transforms with associated latency/throughput costs. Using techniques from convex optimization and network calculus, we will design algorithms that, given a performance budget (e.g., max latency, min throughput), select and parameterize a composition of these primitives to maximize a formal security metric. The system will be evaluated via simulation and a prototype on a cluster using benchmarks from Shenker's prior work on high-performance distributed trust.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic methodology for co-designing performance and security in LANs, moving beyond ad-hoc tuning. It will yield concrete protocols and a tool for network architects to explore the Pareto frontier of this trade-off, directly informing the design of next-generation, secure data-centric networks.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior work that optimizes for performance given security or treats security as a binary feature, Dual explicitly treats security as a continuous, maximizable quantity under a hard performance constraint, leveraging a data-centric network abstraction inspired by Shenker's recent research direction.

ideas.title: Comparative Evaluation of DAHL Against Established Frameworks on Byzantine-Resilient Distributed Consensus
ideas.description: This research implements a Byzantine Fault-Tolerant (BFT) consensus protocol (e.g., a variant of PBFT) in DAHL and, for comparison, in one or two established formal/distributed systems frameworks. The core evaluation involves three phases: formally verifying the DAHL specification for safety and liveness properties under a partial synchrony model; implementing both versions in a simulated network environment; and conducting a rigorous adversarial test campaign with injected network partitions, message delays, drops, and Byzantine node behaviors. The outcome will be a quantitative and qualitative analysis of DAHL's expressiveness, verification capability, and runtime robustness compared to the baseline frameworks, demonstrating its practical value for building verifiable, complex distributed systems.

ideas.title: Dual: Centralized simulator for distributed DAHL

ideas.title: Implement DAHL for Economic-Substance-Aware Distributed Systems
ideas.description: This research applies the DAHL (Distributed Abstract Higher-Order Logic) framework to model and implement complex distributed systems whose core correctness properties are defined by their underlying economic substance—such as value flows, incentive alignments, and de facto work relationships—instead of solely by their statutory or formal algorithmic purpose. By inverting the focus, DAHL specifications would enforce that the system's execution traces are valid not just according to message-passing logic, but according to a formal model of economic reality (e.g., ensuring a gig work platform's matching algorithm cannot create de facto employment relationships if the specification forbids it). This enables the rigorous design of distributed systems that are provably correct with respect to both functional behavior and their intended socio-economic structure, moving beyond technical consensus to guarantee contractual and regulatory compliance by construction.

ideas.title: Applying Ecological Succession Principles to Evolve Complex DAHL Systems
ideas.description: This research seed proposes applying the ecological concept of 'succession' to implement more complex distributed systems in DAHL. In ecology, complex ecosystems develop through a predictable sequence of species colonization and replacement, building stability from simple beginnings. The analogous solution involves guiding this process with general principles rather than a fixed blueprint. Adapted to DAHL, this means creating a framework where complex distributed systems (like sophisticated coordination services or storage layers) are not implemented monolithically. Instead, a set of simple, composable 'pioneer' protocols is defined alongside a rule-based 'succession' framework. Starting from a basic state, the system would automatically evolve through staged integration of more sophisticated components, where each new layer (e.g., consensus, sharding) builds upon and is enabled by the established prior layer. This approach provides a principled, robust pathway for bootstrapping complexity in DAHL systems, improving adaptability and resilience.

ideas.title: Robust Stability Proofs for Protocol Backoff Classes under Model Uncertainty
ideas.description: This research develops a robust stability framework for contention resolution protocols, moving beyond proofs for specific backoff functions. It formally defines classes of admissible backoff functions and bounded model perturbations (e.g., traffic non-stationarity, feedback errors). Using techniques from stochastic hybrid systems and robust control, it proves that protocols employing functions from these classes guarantee stability (e.g., mean-square stability, ISS) within the defined perturbation bounds. The result is a set of verifiable design criteria for backoff functions that ensure stable operation in realistic, non-ideal network conditions.

ideas.title: Minimal Backoff Scaling for Stability in Interpolated Finite-to-Infinite User Contention Models
ideas.description: This research creates a unified model that interpolates between the finite-user and infinite-user regimes in random access networks, such as CSMA/CA. Within this interpolated framework, it seeks to solve the dual problem of finding the minimal required growth rate of the backoff function (e.g., linear, exponential) that guarantees system stability for a given target load. The core novelty is characterizing the precise transition in these minimal backoff requirements as the system scales, answering how stability constraints relax or tighten on the path from a small, known number of contenders to a large, unpredictable population.

ideas.title: Rigorous Stability Analysis for Heterogeneous Backoff Functions in Random Access Networks
ideas.description: This research proposes to develop a rigorous mathematical framework to prove stability for random access networks where different user classes employ distinct, potentially non-linear, backoff functions. It aims to establish formal conditions—such as constraints on the mix of function classes and their parameters—under which the heterogeneous system remains stable, generalizing prior single-function proofs. The analysis will investigate how interactions between different backoff strategies (e.g., polynomial vs. exponential) affect global stability, potentially identifying dominant function classes or dangerous combinations. This provides a foundational theory for designing and certifying stable, heterogeneous MAC protocols, a critical step beyond simulation-based or homogeneous-system analyses.

ideas.title: Minimal Stability-Constrained Backoff in Stochastic Two-User Contention Games
ideas.description: This research synthesizes a two-user stochastic game model of random access contention with the dual optimization problem of finding a minimal backoff growth rate for stability. The core objective is to characterize the set of backoff functions that are both Nash equilibria of the strategic interaction and satisfy a rigorous stochastic stability constraint at a target load. This reveals the fundamental trade-off between protocol efficiency (minimal backoff) and strategic resilience, identifying the boundary where a stable system becomes vulnerable to selfish user deviations. The result is a game-theoretically robust stability criterion for protocol design, moving beyond average-case analysis to guarantee performance under strategic behavior.

ideas.title: Mean-Field Embedded Dyadic Stochastic Games for Network-Aware Strategic Analysis
ideas.description: This approach integrates a detailed two-user stochastic game into a mean-field game to analyze strategic interactions within large populations. The dyadic game models the precise, stateful interaction between two strategic agents, while the mean-field component captures the influence and feedback from the broader user network, including congestion, imitation, and popularity effects. This allows for the derivation of tractable mean-field equilibria that account for both microscopic strategic nuance and macroscopic emergent phenomena. The resulting framework provides robust predictions of system stability and agent behavior that are scalable and relevant to real multi-user platforms.

ideas.description: This research investigates the stability boundaries of network protocols that utilize hybrid or non-acknowledgment-based feedback for congestion control. It specifically analyzes how the stability region contracts or expands as the protocol's backoff function deviates from exponential, moving through near-exponential and fast-linear forms. The core novelty is characterizing the precise interaction between the type and quality of the feedback signal and the permissible shape of the backoff function to maintain system stability. This provides a unified theoretical framework for designing next-generation, stable protocols that can safely employ aggressive backoff and alternative feedback mechanisms.

ideas.title: Predicting the Finite-Size Scaling Horizon: From Asymptotics to Practical System Design
ideas.description: This research develops a framework to bridge the gap between asymptotic infinite-population theory and the design of real, large-scale finite systems. It focuses on predicting the 'scaling horizon'—the specific, finite population size at which a system's behavior effectively converges to its asymptotic limit within a defined engineering tolerance. The approach rigorously derives and validates first- and second-order finite-size corrections for key performance metrics across models like communication networks, distributed consensus, and sampling processes. The outcome is a practical methodology for determining when asymptotic laws are applicable and for sizing systems to reliably operate near their theoretical limits.

ideas.title: Lyapunov-Function-Based Stability Proof for Backoff Protocols via Ecological Population Dynamics Analogy
ideas.description: This research seed proposes adapting a solution from theoretical ecology to provide a rigorous stability proof for specific backoff functions in network protocols. In ecology, the global asymptotic stability of equilibrium populations in competitive systems is often proven using Lyapunov function techniques, moving beyond simulation or local linear analysis. The analogous solution involves constructing a scalar function of the system state that consistently decreases toward an equilibrium point. Adapted to the networking context, the research would formally model the stochastic dynamics of a contention-based protocol (like CSMA/CA with a specific backoff function), define a suitable Lyapunov function (e.g., based on queue lengths or expected delays), and mathematically prove that its expected drift is negative, thereby delivering the rigorous stability proof that the original idea sought.

ideas.title: Pre-asymptotic scaling and phase transitions in finite-to-infinite user population models
ideas.description: This research develops a framework to map the landscape of system behavior as a function of the number of users (N), from small finite values through the extremely large but finite regime, and finally to the infinite limit. It focuses on identifying and mathematically characterizing distinct scaling phases—such as linear, polynomial, and logarithmic regimes—and the critical population sizes at which transitions between these phases occur. The work will derive precise error bounds for using infinite-population approximations on finite systems, specifying the N required for a given accuracy. This provides a practical, predictive theory for system design and analysis that bridges abstract theory and real-world, large-scale implementations.

ideas.title: Queueing-Theoretic Analysis of Policy Application Delays and Purposive Drift
ideas.description: This research applies queueing network theory to model the internal corporate 'policy application system,' where employee requests or incidents (e.g., approvals, grievances) are customers and managers/HR are servers. It then integrates the purposive classification framework to analyze how prolonged waiting times and backlog distributions cause the *de facto* application of a policy to deviate from its *de jure* purpose. The novel synergy quantifies 'purposive drift' as a function of system congestion, providing managers with a diagnostic tool to see if delays are causing unintended, systematic bias in rule enforcement, and to design queues that align with policy goals rather than undermine them.

ideas.title: Mapping the Stochastic Stability Phase Transition for Parametric Backoff Functions in Contention Systems
ideas.description: This research constructs a rigorous framework to characterize the stability boundary for backoff functions within a well-defined stochastic system model (e.g., a many-user random access channel or a queueing system with contention). The core method involves defining a Lyapunov function for the system state and analyzing the conditional drift. For a rich family of parameterized backoff functions (spanning sub-linear to super-exponential), the study performs a bifurcation analysis, identifying the critical parameter manifold where the drift condition fails. The output is a phase diagram that delineates stable and unstable regimes as a function of both backoff function parameters and offered load, providing general design principles for robust protocol design.

ideas.title: Empirically-Grounded Signatures of Regime Transition in Socio-Technical Systems
ideas.description: This research develops a theoretically rigorous yet empirically anchored framework that characterizes the transition between finite and infinite-user regimes in socio-technical systems (like AI platforms or online markets). The core innovation is the derivation of specific, measurable 'transition signatures'—predictions about how key metrics (e.g., performance variance, adaptation speed, or resource contention) must scale and change as a function of user population size. The framework is validated by testing these signatures against data from large-scale simulations, existing platform datasets, and controlled experiments, ensuring the interpolation model describes not just a mathematical limit but observable dynamical behavior.

ideas.title: Formally Verified Hybrid-Feedback Transport Control Resilient to Adversarial Signal Manipulation
ideas.description: This research designs and analyzes a transport protocol that cross-validates traditional end-to-end acknowledgments with in-network telemetry signals (e.g., INT or advanced ECN) to detect and mitigate adversarial feedback manipulation. A formal control-theoretic framework is developed to prove stability conditions for this hybrid feedback system under defined threat models, including loss or corruption of either signal path. The protocol's performance and resilience are then empirically validated against standard TCP variants in network emulations featuring adversarial middlebox behaviors and targeted signal attacks.

ideas.title: Robust, Data-Driven Queueing Analysis for Backlog and Delay Prediction Under Model Ambiguity
ideas.description: This research develops a hybrid analytical-empirical framework for backlog analysis that overcomes the fragility of classical queueing assumptions. It first employs statistical and machine learning techniques to characterize the true, often non-standard, stochastic processes from system data without imposing parametric forms. These learned models then feed into a robust queueing network analysis, which computes guaranteed bounds on delays and backlog distributions under the worst-case behavior within an ambiguity set derived from the data. The result is a practical tool that provides reliable, risk-aware performance predictions for complex systems where traditional queueing models fail.

ideas.title: The Algorithmic Purposive Audit: Deconstructing Flawed Classifications in Automated Workplace Governance
ideas.description: This research proposes a novel 'purposive audit' framework for the algorithmic systems that apply and enforce internal corporate policies. It starts from the axiom that the rule classifications programmed into these systems (e.g., categorizing employees, behaviors, or risk levels) are inherently flawed, often importing irrational legislative biases or creating new, opaque distortions. By applying a purposive analysis—asking what legitimate operational goal a classification truly serves—at the micro-level of code and data models, the research develops tools to identify, map, and propose alternative, more rational structures for workplace governance. This shifts the critique from abstract legal theory to a practical, engineering-oriented intervention in the digital private sphere where rules are increasingly instantiated and automated.

ideas.title: Active-Disentangled Inference of MAC Backoff Protocols via Controlled Contention Perturbation
ideas.description: This research designs a method to uniquely identify the class of backoff functions (e.g., Binary Exponential, Linear, Polynomial) governing a wireless network by moving beyond passive observation. The core innovation is an active probing framework where a trusted observer node injects precise, minimal contention perturbations—such as a short burst of packets or a deliberate silence—into the channel. By analyzing the statistical deviation of the observed traffic before, during, and after these controlled stimuli, the method constructs a response fingerprint that is highly specific to the underlying backoff mechanics. This active experimentation transforms an ill-posed inverse problem into a well-constrained system identification task, enabling robust and verifiable inference even in noisy, heterogeneous environments.

ideas.title: Inferring Backoff Functions via Spectral Analysis of Contention Traces
ideas.description: This research adapts the inverse problem solution from helioseismology to network contention analysis. In helioseismology, the internal structure of the Sun is inferred from the oscillatory power spectrum of its surface. Analogously, the 'internal' backoff algorithm used by contending nodes is hidden, but manifests in the temporal structure of the observed channel traffic—the sequence of transmissions, collisions, and idle periods. The adapted idea is to design methods that apply spectral analysis (e.g., Fourier transform, wavelet analysis, or autocorrelation) to this contention trace. The resulting spectral signature or correlation decay pattern will be characteristic of the backoff function's class (e.g., binary exponential, linear, or polynomial backoff), allowing us to solve the inverse problem of inferring the backoff scaling law from purely observational data.

ideas.title: Judicial Stability Under Load: Quantifying the Minimal Rhetorical Backoff from Purposive Doctrine
ideas.description: This research applies control-theoretic and queueing concepts from system stability to legal analysis. It posits that courts rhetorically commit to a purposive approach (a high-capacity, complex 'processor') but strategically back off to more textualist or formalist methods (a lower-capacity, simpler 'processor') as caseload, political scrutiny, or doctrinal complexity increases. The core empirical question becomes identifying the minimal rate of this rhetorical backoff—quantified through computational text analysis of judicial opinions—required to keep the legal system stable and predictable at a given 'load,' rather than merely testing if purposivism is lip service. This frames judicial rhetoric as a dynamic, stability-preserving feedback mechanism.

ideas.title: Stability Analysis in Heterogeneous Backoff Networks via Ecological Niche Theory
ideas.description: This research seed proposes analyzing system stability in contention-based networks (e.g., WiFi, Aloha) when different user stations employ fundamentally different classes of backoff functions (e.g., exponential vs. polynomial backoff). Drawing an analogy from ecology, specifically the theory of niche differentiation and the Lotka-Volterra competition model, the problem is reframed as one of species coexistence. The analogous ecological solution involves finding the parameter space (competition coefficients, carrying capacities) that allows multiple species to stably share a limited resource. Adapted to the networking context, the research will involve developing a mathematical model, likely using mean-field or drift analysis, to represent the interaction between heterogeneous user classes. The core objective is to derive the necessary and sufficient conditions on the mix of backoff functions and their parameters (like aggressiveness) that guarantee system stability, fair resource sharing, and robust performance, thereby challenging the standard assumption of homogeneous backoff strategies.

ideas.title: Heterogeneous Adjudicative Backoff: A Stability Analysis of Constitutional Equality Rights
ideas.description: This research applies the analytical framework of heterogeneous backoff functions from distributed network stability to constitutional law. It models claimants from different social groups (distinguished by the 'rational/pragmatic' framework) as agents using distinct strategic functions to determine when and how to re-initiate Section 15 Charter challenges after an adverse ruling. By analyzing this system's stability, the work can formally identify whether current judicial doctrines for equality rights create a stable equilibrium that progressively realizes substantive equality, or if they induce instability where certain groups are systematically discouraged from seeking redress, thereby violating the constitutional promise of equal benefit and protection of the law.

ideas.title: Adapting Molecular Reaction Kinetics to Two-User Stochastic Games
ideas.description: This research adapts the solution methodology from chemical kinetics for bimolecular reactions to the two-user stochastic game. The analogous problem—modeling the stochastic interaction of two molecular species—is solved using a master equation that defines transition propensities between discrete population states. We adapt this by defining the joint state space of two strategic users (e.g., cooperation/defection levels or resource states). Each possible strategic interaction becomes a 'reaction' with a transition rate (propensity) determined by a game-theoretic payoff function and a behavioral rule (e.g., logit response). The adapted solution involves formulating the game's Chemical Master Equation, then using analytical solving for small state spaces or a Gillespie-type agent-based simulation to analyze the probability distribution over states, identify stochastic equilibria, and compute performance metrics like the mean time to system failure or cooperation. This provides a novel, rigorously stochastic framework for two-user game analysis rooted in physical science methods.

ideas.title: Diachronic Multi-Source Modeling of Statutory Purpose Evolution
ideas.description: This research system moves beyond a static textual analysis to computationally model how statutory purpose is constructed and evolves. It ingests and aligns temporal data streams including legislative history documents, the statute's text, and a time-stamped corpus of relevant jurisprudence. By applying NLP for semantic change tracking and network analysis on citation and argument patterns, the system identifies key junctures where interpretations of purpose shift, diverge, or solidify. This provides an objective, evidence-based map of the dynamic and often contested nature of statutory purpose, offering insights into legislative intent, judicial activism, and doctrinal stability.

ideas.title: A Queueing-Theoretic Model for Analyzing Purposive Adjudication Backlogs in Labour Law
ideas.description: This research synthesizes queueing theory and purposive analysis to model the adjudication system for contentious labour law scope cases (e.g., gig workers, dependent contractors) as a processing network. It quantifies how delays in resolving these legally ambiguous cases create backlogs, affecting worker rights and regulatory certainty. The model incorporates the 'purposive weight' of different case types—based on their alignment with the legislative purpose and potential impact—to analyze how prioritization strategies influence overall system efficiency and the fulfillment of the law's core objectives. This enables evidence-based recommendations for tribunal case management and judicial administration to reduce bottlenecks in clarifying legal coverage.

ideas.title: Open Population Dynamics in Finite User Models via Ecological Metapopulation Theory
ideas.description: This research seed challenges the static finite population assumption common in many user models (e.g., in queueing theory, peer-to-peer networks, or online platforms) by introducing dynamic entry and exit. Drawing an analogy from ecology's metapopulation theory, which solves the analogous problem of modeling species presence in patchy habitats with constant colonization and extinction, we adapt its core formalism. The adapted idea treats the user base as an open metapopulation: a finite set of system 'slots' (patches) can be occupied or vacant. User arrival is modeled as a colonization process driven by external influx and peer influence, while departure is an extinction process driven by churn. This allows for the analytical or simulation-based study of active user dynamics, equilibrium states, and resilience, moving beyond a fixed N to a model where the effective population size is a time-varying outcome of intrinsic arrival and departure rates.

ideas.title: Automated Lyapunov Function Synthesis Integrated with Drift Analysis for Provable Stability Regions
ideas.description: This research integrates classical Lyapunov drift analysis with modern computational techniques to automate the most difficult step: finding a valid Lyapunov function. The system employs a two-phase approach: first, it uses data-driven exploration or formal optimization (e.g., SOS programming) to generate candidate Lyapunov functions that satisfy necessary conditions within a hypothesized region. Second, it applies rigorous Lyapunov drift analysis to these candidates to either formally verify stability or provide counter-examples to refine the search. This creates a closed-loop, computationally-assisted proof system that makes rigorous stability certification feasible for complex systems where purely analytical approaches fail.

ideas.title: Adapt Ecological Resilience Analysis via Lyapunov Functions for Network Stability Proofs
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from Theoretical Ecology, where Lyapunov stability techniques are used to prove the resilience and domain of attraction for complex ecosystems near a stable equilibrium. The analogous solution involves constructing a Lyapunov function from system parameters (like interaction strengths) to define a rigorous 'basin of stability'. Adapted to the original research context, we treat a networked or algorithmic system as an ecological community. By constructing a suitable Lyapunov function from the system's inherent structure (e.g., a quadratic form based on a Laplacian or a system Hamiltonian) and performing a drift analysis, we can provide rigorous, formal proofs for the conjectured stability regions, moving beyond heuristic or simulation-based arguments.

ideas.title: Inferring Policy Diffusion Mechanisms from Observed Jurisdictional Contention
ideas.description: This research develops a formal inverse problem framework to uncover the hidden diffusion policies that guide the adoption of labor law interpretations across court systems. By analogizing jurisdictions to network nodes contending to establish legal precedent (observed through citation patterns and ruling outcomes), the method applies statistical inference techniques from channel contention analysis to estimate the underlying 'backoff' and 'retransmission' functions—such as the influence of court hierarchy, ideological distance, or case salience—that best explain the observed diffusion data. This enables a data-driven, mechanism-based theory of legal policy diffusion, moving beyond descriptive network mapping to causal inference about the procedural rules of inter-jurisdictional influence.

ideas.title: Legislative Classification as a Noisy Dataset: A Machine Learning Approach to Coverage Modeling
ideas.description: This research idea adapts a solution from machine learning for handling noisy datasets to the problem of flawed legislative classifications. In ML, models are often trained on data with imperfect labels, and solutions involve techniques to look beyond the initial categorization to discover the true underlying structure. Here, we treat the entire corpus of legislation and its associated case law as a dataset where the legal classifications (e.g., definitions of eligibility, liability, or protected classes) are the initial 'noisy labels.' The research involves building a computational coverage model that starts by rejecting the rationality of these labels. Using natural language processing and unsupervised learning methods (like topic modeling or embedding-based clustering), the model will analyze the factual scenarios, outcomes, and statutory language to cluster legal instances based on latent functional similarities, not the legislatively imposed categories. The output is a new, empirically derived map of legal coverage that highlights where the original legislative taxonomy is misaligned with reality—revealing both unprotected groups that functionally belong and overprotected scenarios that do not, thereby providing a data-driven basis for legal critique and reform.

ideas.title: Modeling the Noisy Channel of Legal Communication: Testing the Stability of Purposive Interpretation Under Informational Constraints
ideas.description: This research investigates whether the observed instability or rhetorical dominance of purposive statutory interpretation in court decisions can be modeled as a problem of communication under noisy or time-varying channel capacity. It applies concepts from information theory and algorithm stability to the legal system, where the 'channel' is the transmission of legislative intent through text, history, and precedent, and its 'capacity' is affected by factors like aging statutes, complex records, and evolving societal values. The project will empirically test if deviations from a stable, genuine purposive analysis correlate quantitatively with measurable increases in informational noise or constraint, moving beyond a binary assessment of 'lip service' to a diagnostic model of interpretive failure.

ideas.title: Minimal Critical Damping in Queueing Systems
ideas.description: This idea applies the concept of critical damping from control theory to the dual problem of minimizing backoff subject to a stability constraint. In mechanical vibration analysis, the critical damping coefficient is the minimal damping that suppresses oscillations and ensures the fastest non-oscillatory return to equilibrium under a driving force. Analogously, in a queueing or networked system (e.g., communication protocols), we treat the arrival/workload as the driving force and the backoff policy as the damping. The goal is to derive the minimal growth rate or shape of the backoff function (e.g., exponential, polynomial) that just guarantees system stability (e.g., positive recurrence, bounded queue lengths) at a specified target load, preventing instability (queue blow-up) without being overly conservative. This involves modeling the system dynamics, defining a suitable stability criterion (like drift analysis of a Lyapunov function), and solving an optimization problem to find the minimal backoff parameters that satisfy it.

ideas.title: Empirically Grounded and Validated Agent-Based Modeling for Robust Heterogeneous Scenario Analysis
ideas.description: This approach embeds agent-based modeling within a rigorous empirical workflow to ensure scientific robustness. It begins by using inverse methods and experimental data to calibrate agent decision rules, then performs extensive global sensitivity analysis to map how assumptions drive outcomes. The core simulation is only used for scenario exploration after passing validation tests against independent empirical benchmarks. This creates a closed loop of calibration, validation, and analysis, transforming the ABM from a speculative storytelling tool into a reliable instrument for studying complex, heterogeneous behaviors.

ideas.title: Adaptive Control for Communication Algorithms under Uncertain Channel Capacity
ideas.description: This research seed investigates algorithm stability when channel capacity is not constant or perfectly known. Drawing an analogy from Control Systems Engineering, specifically adaptive control solutions for systems with uncertain parameters, we propose to adapt these principles. The analogous solution uses techniques like Model Reference Adaptive Control (MRAC) to estimate unknown system parameters online and adjust the control law for stability. Adapted to this context, the research would design communication algorithms that incorporate a real-time estimator for the channel's instantaneous capacity. The algorithm's core operations—such as its coding scheme, modulation order, or power allocation—would then be dynamically tuned based on this running estimate. This creates a feedback loop where the algorithm adapts to the channel's variations, aiming to guarantee stability and robust performance despite the inherent uncertainty and non-stationarity, moving beyond the limiting assumption of a fixed, known capacity.

ideas.title: An Institutional-Computational Framework for Modeling Legal Coverage Disputes
ideas.description: This research develops a hybrid, empirically informed framework to model strategic interactions in legal coverage disputes. It moves beyond pure game theory by integrating three components: an institutional rule-coder, a bounded-rationality agent-based model, and NLP analysis of historical case language. This allows the model to simulate how specific procedural settings, social learning, and the ambiguity of legal terms themselves shape dispute outcomes. The result is a robust, testable system that can generate predictions about dispute resolution under proposed legal reforms or changing economic conditions.

ideas.title: Adaptive Backoff Tuning for Stability under Non-Stationary Load
ideas.description: This research develops an online control system that dynamically finds and maintains the minimal backoff policy required for system stability. Instead of solving a static optimization for a fixed target load, the system continuously observes a real-time stability signal and adjusts the aggressiveness of its backoff function. This allows it to automatically adapt to unknown, shifting load patterns and unmodeled system dynamics. The core challenge is designing a provably safe adaptive algorithm that minimizes performance overhead (backoff) while guaranteeing stability with high probability under non-stationary conditions.

ideas.title: Inverse Inference of Legislative Intent via Bayesian Deconvolution of Judicial Coverage
ideas.description: This research adapts the inverse problem of astronomical image deconvolution to legal analysis. In astrophysics, a telescope's point spread function blurs a true light source; deconvolution algorithms use the observed image and a model of the blur to reconstruct the original source. Analogously, we propose that the pattern of judicial decisions on a law's coverage (the 'observed image') results from the convolution of the original legislative intent (the 'true source') with a 'judicial interpretation function' embodying legal reasoning principles. By modeling this function—using factors like textualism/purposivism tendencies, precedent networks, and canons of construction—we can apply Bayesian deconvolution techniques. This allows us to statistically infer the most likely legislative intent that, when filtered through the judicial system, produces the empirically observed coverage landscape, thereby scaling the deduction of intent from individual cases to systemic patterns.

ideas.title: Stability Analysis Using Ecological Population Feedback Models
ideas.description: This research seed challenges the standard assumption that transmission control protocols must rely primarily on acknowledgment-based feedback. Drawing an analogy from theoretical ecology, specifically predator-prey dynamics where stability emerges from a mix of direct consumption and indirect environmental cues, we propose studying TCP (or similar protocol) stability under hybrid feedback mechanisms. The analogous ecological solution involves modeling and analyzing systems with combined immediate and delayed feedback channels. Adapted to networking, this means designing and analyzing control laws that use traditional ACKs alongside non-acknowledgment-based signals—such as periodic, aggregate link utilization reports from routers or statistically significant shifts in measured base RTT—as a secondary, stabilizing input. The core research would involve developing a analytical framework, likely using delay differential equations or hybrid systems theory, to characterize the stability region and performance trade-offs of such hybrid feedback protocols, potentially leading to more robust designs for environments with high loss, asymmetric paths, or ACK compression.

ideas.title: Online Power Allocation for Reliable Streaming Under Unknown, Time-Varying Channel Capacity
ideas.description: This research formulates the problem of transmitting a data stream over a channel with unknown and time-varying capacity as an online optimization task with a strict total energy budget. The core objective is to minimize regret—the gap in total successfully transmitted data between the proposed algorithm and an ideal oracle that knows the future capacity curve. The system design explicitly decouples the challenge of learning the noisy, varying capacity from other signal processing issues, enabling the development and rigorous evaluation of adaptive algorithms that dynamically adjust transmission power and coding rate based on noisy channel feedback to maximize throughput under uncertainty.

ideas.title: Adapting Epidemiological Compartmental Models for Agent-Based Behavioral Simulation
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from epidemiology to the context of studying complex, heterogeneous user behaviors. In epidemiology, compartmental models like SIR are used in agent-based simulations to understand disease spread in populations with varied contact networks and susceptibility. The analogous solution involves defining clear agent states and probabilistic transition rules between them based on local interactions. Adapted to behavioral research, this method involves designing an agent-based model where users occupy distinct behavioral states (e.g., Unaware, Evaluator, Adopter, Lapsed). Transition rules between these states are defined by functions of an agent's personal attributes, social network exposure to other agents' states, and environmental stimuli. This provides a robust, structured framework to simulate and analyze the emergent diffusion patterns of technologies, misinformation, or organizational practices that cannot be captured by analytical, aggregate models alone.

ideas.title: A Lyapunov Stability Framework for the Dynamic Evolution of Purposive Labour Law Interpretation
ideas.description: This research applies Lyapunov drift analysis from control theory to the systematic, purposive interpretation of labour law scope. It constructs a formal dynamical system model where the state is the legal interpretation (e.g., the scope of 'employee'), the 'control input' is new judicial reasoning from applying the purposive framework to novel cases, and the 'Lyapunov function' represents the system's adherence to core statutory purpose. The analysis rigorously proves whether the purposive system, when perturbed by new factual scenarios, drifts towards a stable, coherent region of interpretation or diverges into legal uncertainty, providing a mathematical foundation for predicting the long-term robustness of purposive legal doctrines.

ideas.title: Modeling the Quasistationary Dynamics of Judicial Policy Diffusion in Labor Law
ideas.description: This research develops a formal analytical model to characterize the quasistationary behavior of judicial interpretations of labor law scope as they diffuse across jurisdictions. By treating the network of courts and legal systems as an infinite population, the model seeks to identify the metastable equilibria—persistent but not permanent states—where certain interpretations become dominant before a potential regime shift. This allows for the prediction of convergence points, resistance thresholds, and the conditions under which novel legal doctrines might achieve systemic stability, providing a mathematical framework for the policy diffusion process.

ideas.title: Adapting Pharmacokinetic Compartment Models for Backlog Dynamics
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution to a parallel problem in pharmacology—modeling the flow of a drug through the body using compartmental models—to the context of backlog analysis. In pharmacokinetics, compartments represent physiological states, and differential equations model the transfer rates between them to predict concentration delays. Here, we model a system's workflow as a network of queueing compartments (e.g., incoming, in-progress, blocked). By developing analogous transfer rate equations between these states, we can analyze the dynamics of work accumulation, predict backlog distributions over time, and pinpoint which specific process stages act as metabolic 'bottlenecks' causing delays, providing a dynamic and predictive alternative to static queueing models.

ideas.title: Historical Pathology of Purposive Failure in Corporate Rule Application
ideas.description: This research conducts a historical analysis of judicial failures in statutory purposive interpretation to develop a diagnostic taxonomy of failure modes. This taxonomy is then applied to analyze internal corporate rule systems, examining how analogous interpretive pathologies manifest in workplace policy application, enforcement, and adjudication. The study aims to identify recurring patterns where the original purpose of a corporate rule is systematically undermined by formalistic, inconsistent, or biased application at the micro-level. By tracing the historical evolution of purposive breakdowns in law to their modern corporate parallels, the research provides organizations with a framework to audit and redesign internal governance systems to prevent these predictable failures.
